entry.title: Re: Journal Entry Number Range From Number Update
entry.content:
Good day @ACLyn 

Thank you for your question.

In practice, once you have used the journal entry number for postings in the fiscal year, the “From Number” field is generally no longer editable. That is why it appears greyed out in the app.

Consult these references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/4460d7531a4d424de10000000a174cb4.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/1fe9ab02f1114619a13a7df8ed87ab9b.html?locale=en-US

I suggest you take the path of reviewing the current interval design and if you require, consider maintaining a valid alternative configuration in your configuration environment.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Key User Adaptation: Powerful New Features & Seamless Transition to Developer Extensibility
entry.content:
With key user adaptation, you can change the user interface of SAP Fiori apps directly in the Fiori launchpad — intuitively and without having to write new code. The changes are upgrade-safe adaptations to SAP Fiori apps. Key users can open the UI Adaptation mode for a Fiori app directly from their user menu in the Fiori launchpad (menu entry Adapt UI).

A key user is a business expert who is authorized to make changes on behalf of other users — for example a team member who is interested in technology. The goal of key user extensibility is to empower business users to be able to adapt their working environment to suit their needs, without having to rely on system administrators.

 

What can key users change with key user adaptation?

Key users can perform the following changes in Fiori apps based on SAP Fiori elements:

  • Hide/show the information that shall be visible in the object page header
  • Hide/show tabs, sections, tables, or action buttons on the object page
  • Define the fields visible in forms on the object page
  • Set the configuration for tables (select all, selection mode, thresholds, view settings, export, etc.)
  • Set the configuration for the filter bar (clear button, usage of the semantic date range control)
  • Set the configuration for the complete list report / object page (variant management, initial load)
  • Change Text Arrangement (ID only, Text first, Text last, Text only)
  • Change Labels (global label changes)
  • Rename (only for a dedicated field – applies only locally to a form field)
  • Define the fields visible in the mass edit dialog. In the key user adaptation mode, all fields except static read-only fields are available.
  • And more

The scope was continuously enhanced. The bold items above are extremely powerful new features available since S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2508 / S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition 2025 / On-Premise 2025. They are described in detail in the following sections.

 

New feature: configuration for tables

The Configuration functionality is available in key user adaptation mode when the context menu entry Configuration is selected on a table, on the filter bar, or on page level. The context menu on a table contains — for example in Fiori elements V4 — the following entries:

 

Among other things, the following settings can be configured here:

  • Enable Table Export — configures whether the Export button is displayed in the table.
  • Frozen Column Count — configures the count of columns that are always visible when scrolling horizontally.
  • Personalization — configures whether the table personalization settings are enabled. To configure the listed settings individually, choose Own Settings.
  • Row Count — configures the number of rows to be displayed in the table.
  • Width Including Column Header — configures whether the column header label is considered when calculating the column width.
  • Enable Select All — configures whether the Select All option is displayed in the table.
  • Selection Limit — configures the maximum limit of the number of rows that can be selected at once (e.g., by using Select All).
  • Threshold — determines the maximum number of entries loaded when the application is started. Scroll Threshold determines the maximum number loaded during scrolling if new entries need to be fetched. Increasing these settings will lead to an increased initial load time but deliver a smoother scroll experience.

Note: Not all settings listed above are available for all table types and page types. Furthermore, there are differences between Fiori elements V2 and Fiori elements V4 (Fiori elements V2 offers fewer settings).

Example – List Report Grid Table in Fiori elements V4:

 

 

New feature: configuration for the filter bar

The Configuration context menu entry on the filter bar opens settings for the filter bar. Among other things, the following settings can be configured here:

  • Display Clear Button — configures whether the Clear button is displayed in the filter bar.
  • Enable Semantic Date Range menu options — configures that single date range filters offer semantic operators (like "Today", "This Month", "Last Year").

Example – List Report Filter Bar in Fiori elements V4:

  

 

New feature: configuration for the complete list report / object page

The Configuration context menu entry at page level opens settings for the entire List Report or Object Page. Among other things, the following settings can be configured here:

  • Variant Management (Table Variant Management) — configures how variant management is used on the List Report page or in tables on the Object Page.
  • Initial Load — configures how data is loaded initially when the app is opened.

Example – List Report in Fiori elements V4:

 

 

New feature: change text arrangement

The Change Text Arrangement functionality is available in key user adaptation mode when the context menu entry Change Text Arrangement is selected on a table, on the filter bar, or on a form.

This powerful new functionality allows changing centrally the text arrangement for all relevant fields in a Fiori app. A key user can decide for each field whether it shall be shown as:

Option

Example (Company Code "0001" / "SAP AG")

ID only

0001

Text only

SAP AG

Text first

SAP AG (0001)

Text last

0001 (SAP AG)

Note: These changes are independent of specific controls. You change the text arrangement for the underlying OData property. If a specific field or control does not use the text arrangement from the OData property, the changes will not be applied to that control.

 

 

New feature: change labels

The Change Labels functionality is available in key user adaptation mode when the context menu entry Change Labels is selected on a table, on the filter bar, or on a form.

This allows changing the labels centrally for all relevant fields in a Fiori app: if an identical label is available in the filter bar, in tables, forms, and charts of a Fiori application, then all places are changed consistently. This is the key difference from the existing Rename feature, which only applies locally to a form field.

Example: You can consistently change the text "Material" to "Product" across your entire Fiori app.

Note: These changes are independent of specific controls. You change the label for the underlying OData property. If a specific field or control does not use the text from the OData property, the changes will not be applied to that control.

 

 

Need more complex changes? Seamless Transition from Key User to Developer Extensibility.

If you need to make more complex changes — for example, adding fragments to visualize data as a chart, changing a responsive table to a grid table, or making the app available to different business roles, user groups, organizational units, and countries — you can extend an application by creating an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project (developer adaptation).

When creating an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project, you can select the brand-new option Import Key User Changes (available since S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602; not yet available for S/4HANA Private Cloud / On-Premise) to import all key user changes made so far. This means key user changes will not be lost — they become part of the new SAPUI5 Adaptation Project.

 

For an introduction to Adaptation Projects, see Work with SAPUI5 Adaptation Projects to Make Changes and Extend the Source Code of an Application Variant of an SAP Fiori Application in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

For more detailed information, see Extending SAP Fiori Applications.

 

Prerequisites

Public Cloud:

  • The IAM app F6184_TRAN provides the relevant authorizations. It is included in the business catalogs SAP_CORE_BC_EXT_FLX_CUS_PC and SAP_CORE_BC_EXT_FLX_PC (see Adapting SAP Fiori UIs at Runtime - Key User Adaptation).

Private Cloud / On-Premise:

  • Your administrator assigned you to the authorization role SAP_UI_FLEX_KEY_USER (see Enabling Key User Adaptation).

General requirements:

  • You run your SAPUI5 app on a desktop computer or laptop.
  • Your app is enabled for key user adaptation.
  • A minimum screen size of 800×600 is required for key user adaptation. Anything smaller can affect the toolbar elements, causing them to overlap.

 

Tips for Key User Adaptation for Fiori Apps in SAP S/4HANA

Where do I make Key User changes?

  • Key users typically make changes in the Development environment.

How do I transport Key User changes?

  • Active changes must be published to make them ready for transport.
  • To publish your changes for transport, you must be assigned a transport request by your technical team.
  • Your technical team manages the transports through to your Production environment.

 

Key takeaways

  • Key User Adaptation lets you make common changes to SAP Fiori apps directly in the Fiori Launchpad — no developer knowledge required.
  • Key User Adaptation is now extremely powerful with new options for:
    • Configuring important UI elements (tables, filter bar, complete list report / object page)
    • Changing the text arrangement of fields
    • Changing labels globally across an entire Fiori app
  • Changes are made in the Development environment and published for transport.
  • Published changes can be transported to other environments by your technical team.
  • Key User Adaptation changes can be imported when creating an SAPUI5 Adaptation Project for more complex changes (S/4HANA Public Cloud 2602+). This allows a seamless transition from key user to developer extensibility.

 

Where can I learn more about Key User Adaptation for Fiori Apps in SAP S/4HANA?

  • SAP Help – Key User Adaptation (S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition): Adapting SAP Fiori UIs at Runtime – Key User Adaptation
  • SAP Help – Key User Adaptation (S/4HANA Cloud Private Edition and On-Premise): Adapting SAP Fiori UIs at Runtime – Key User Adaptation
  • SAP Learning – Tutorials: Key User Adaptation for SAPUI5 Applications
  • SAP Help – SAP Fiori elements V4: Extending Delivered Apps With Key User Adaptation
  • SAP Help – SAP Fiori elements V2: Adapting the UI

entry.title: Re: Is it possible to have different vendor on unplanned...
entry.content:
Hello @Fidelis 

Yes, you can do that using the Create Supplier Invoice-Advanced app. See KBA 3426129 and Entering a Supplier Invoice for more insights.

Regarding your question, can the unplanned delivery cost be posted as part of the material moving average pice:

Yes, the Public Cloud provides this option. However, it depends on how you configure unplanned delivery costs. You can decide whether the unplanned delivery cost is distributed to invoice items or posted to a separate accounting line. If you distribute the cost to stock relevant invoice items for a material that is managed with moving average price, the material valuation is updated accordingly.

Consult these references for more details on unplanned delivery cost distribution:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/634261119fec4d58970471f2c4a9a740/8daa3fa8103347ef9830a8337f69f3bd.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0e602d466b99490187fcbb30d1dc897c/b6c85168201041aaa97bd8e3cb0d9e28.html?locale=en-US

Note that, if your business scenario entails a recurring freight vendor different from the PO vendor, then you should handle such cleanly through planned delivery costs in the purchase order rather than relying on unplanned delivery costs at the time of the invoice. KBA 3548380 highlights the behaviour of delivery cost and its dependency on the freight condition setup.

If your business requirement is only to allow the freight invoice to come from a different vendor and still have the cost absorbed into the material value, then the recommended standard approach is for you to:

  • Use Create Supplier Invoice-Advanced app
  • Enter the relevant PO reference
  • Maintain the different Invoicing Party
  • Ensure you set configuration 101948 so that unplanned delivery costs are distributed to invoice items rather than posted separately.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP Currency conversion rate checking for a particula...
entry.content:
Hi @KVKUMAR ,
SAP stores exchange rates in table TCURR, and the standard way to view or maintain them is through the following transactions:T‑Code: OB08 — Maintain Exchange Rates
This is the most common screen people use. Enter T‑code: OB08 and Press Enter then you will view exchange rates by: Exchange rate type (e.g., M = average rate), From currency / To currency and valid-from date. Check the exact rate used on the date your PR was created.

entry.title: Re: The returns indicator cannot be set for the purchase...
entry.content:
Hi @singh-siddhant ,
My friend you are seeing this error because SAP ECC is blocking the RETPO (returns indicator) for your STO item based on configuration or item type restrictions. The issue is common in intercompany Return STO scenarios and usually comes down to missing configuration or an item category that does not allow returns. Below is a clear, structured way to diagnose and fix it.

Item Category Does Not Allow Returns. SAP Help explicitly states that RETPO cannot be set for:
Service items (Item category D)
Limit items (Item category B)

check in ME21N/ME22N → Item details → Item category. Ensure it is Standard (blank) and not D or B. To Fix change item category to standard Or create a new STO with a standard item category

entry.title: Re: Cash Management Planning & Reporting Requirements for...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you for your question.

Reporting project-level cash flow (inflows and outflows) in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, similar to the CJIA (Project Actual Payment Line Items) report in on-premise, can be achieved using Embedded Analytics with Custom CDS Views. However, there is no standard delivered report in Public Cloud that provides detailed cash flow reporting at the project level.

Standard apps like Project Cost Report (F2513) support plan vs. actual cost analysis and line-item drill-down, but they are limited to cost reporting and do not provide cash flow (payment-based) visibility.

 

To meet this requirement, the recommended approach is to build a custom analytical report using Embedded SAC, combining Finance (Cash Management) data with Project (EPPM) information.

The typical solution involves the following steps.

1. Ensure project assignment in financial postings
You should ensure that all relevant postings carry Project / WBS Element information, including:
Supplier invoices
Customer invoices
Payments
Journal entries

This is essential because:
Cash flow is derived from FI postings, not directly from projects

2. Configure Liquidity Items (Cash Flow Driver)
Cash flow in S/4HANA Cloud is managed via Liquidity Items in Cash Management.
You should:
Assign liquidity items to G/L accounts
Maintain derivation rules based on:
G/L account
Document type
This enables classification of postings into:
Cash Inflow
Cash Outflow

3. Create a Custom CDS View using the Custom CDS Views app
You can use finance-related data sources such as:
I_JournalEntryItem – Journal Entry Line Items
This provides the base data for actual cash movements.
You can also add associations such as:
I_WBSElement – for WBS/Project details
I_Project – for project master data


With this approach, customers can monitor:
Actual cash inflow and outflow per project
Net cash position
Time-based cash movement
CJIA-like payment visibility


Important Notes:
App Project Cost Report (F2513) and Project Cost Report – Line Items (F2538) are the closest standard apps to CJI3 (Actual cost line items)

These apps are cost/revenue-based, not payment-based
Didnt found CJIA (actual payment line items) in standard Public Cloud
Cash flow is handled via Cash Management (Liquidity Items / One Exposure)


With this approach, you can replicate CJIA-like functionality in a cloud-compliant way by combining cash flow data with project assignments.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards
HM Awasthi

entry.title: Re: Change batch of sales order
entry.content:
Hello @li_zhang8ed3ad 

Thank you for your question.

Your research is correct. No, you cannot change the batch number of sales order items by using the Mass Change of Sales Documents app. The mass change functions SAP provide does not include batch as an item change option.

See the following reference for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/a376cd9ea00d476b96f18dea1247e6a5/315edd1c8c1f4675bc68bee977849198.html?locale=en-US

If you need to influence or reselect the batch after you have created a sales order, you will have to handle this through the batch determination process in the sales order or delivery flow, not through mass maintenance. See KBA 3649063 and KBA 3267138 for more insights on supported batch determination fields and its limitations.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Credit check should not happen again, once Sale Order is released
entry.content:
I am creating a sale Order for quantity 10. But the confirmed quantity is 5. Credit check is happening against the Confirmed quantity 5 and blocking the SO. Then the SO is released using VKM3/VKM4. Then while altering the Quantity above 5 or altering the schedule line date, the order is getting Credit blocked again. This should not happen.

So to avoid this from happening we wanted the Credit check to happen for the Order quantity rather than the Confirmed quantity.

To prevent this we tried altering the LV_AMOUNT in  BADI_SD_CM -> Method FSCM_CREDIT_CHECK_ORDER to take the Total Order amount (Logic=  Net Value + Tax ). Even after this, the old behavior based on Confirmed quantity is happening. Can anyone guide how to achieve the above requirement. Also the Credit exposure for the BP has to be updated based on the Order Quantity.

entry.title: Option to disable the REHIRE for ONB, while keeping it active for EC
entry.content:
The customer wants to disable the Rehire process for Onboarding, but keep it for Employee Central.

The set up i know is that Rehire is enabled in Manager Data in the same admin tool as for EC. There is no switch in the system to disable it for ONB module, but keep for EC. Even if all the permissions are removed and there are no users in Rehire dynamic group - the Rehire process keeps triggering for ONB and blocking the process if noone can see the Verification step.

Is there any known set up or workaround to Disable the Rehire just of ONB module?

 

Thank you,

JS

entry.title: Re: Option to disable the REHIRE for ONB, while keeping i...
entry.content:
@YanaStupane Please refer below KBA:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3099039 - How to skip Rehire Verification Process in Onboarding without impacting EC? - Onboarding

NOTE:  This is not a recommended practice and only a suggestion for what can be tried by the customer/partner in their preview environments. The potential issue would be that system will not recognize the rehires in onboarding and would get blocked at Manage Pending Hire with a duplicate national ID, end up hiring someone who is a rehire as a new hire, or even candidate not appearing on Manage Pending Hire. The National Id duplicate check in Manage Pending Hire will trigger despite candidates rehire verification skipping during the onboarding process.

Thanks,

Gopi

entry.title: How to check status of all configuration items (SSCUI) in one view?
entry.content:
Hello,

In S/4HANA Cloud Two-system Landscape (Solution Configuration), the configuration status is managed at the Item level.

In the Solution Configuration – Change Phase screen, we can see the status for each configuration Item (e.g. Open, Confirmed, etc.), but it requires reviewing each item individually to confirm the configuration progress.

For projects with many configuration items (e.g. 200+ SSCUIs), it is difficult to quickly confirm:

・which SSCUIs have already been set to "Confirmed"
・which SSCUIs are still "Open"
・the overall configuration completion status

Currently, the screen only shows the status per item and does not allow checking the status of all configuration steps in a single consolidated view.

Is there any "Standard Way" or "Workaround" to review the status of all configuration items at once?
For example:

・a report
・export/download option
・CBC project overview
・or another app that shows configuration completion status

Any suggestions or best practices used in projects would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: update office users that are being used on the Accoun...

entry.title: Designing the Hiring Process in SmartRecruiters
entry.content:
A well-structured hiring process is critical for ensuring consistency, transparency, and recruitment efficiency. SmartRecruiters provides a highly configurable hiring workflow that allows organizations to design stages, interview steps, approvals, and collaboration processes tailored to their recruitment strategy.

In this blog, we will explore:
• What the Hiring Process is in SmartRecruiters
• Key components of the hiring workflow
• Step-by-step configuration
• A visual architecture diagram of the hiring process

What is the Hiring Process in SmartRecruiters?

The Hiring Process defines the stages a candidate moves through from application to offer or rejection.

It ensures:
• Structured candidate evaluation
• Standardized interview process
• Clear collaboration between recruiters and hiring managers
• Consistent hiring decisions

Core Components of the SmartRecruiters Hiring Process

• New
• In-Review
• Interview
• Offer

Step-by-Step Configuration of Hiring Process in SmartRecruiters

Follow the steps below to design or modify a hiring process.

Step 1: Navigate to Hiring Process Settings
Go to Settings>Select Configuration>Click Hiring Process

Step 2: Create or Edit a Hiring Process
Click Create Hiring Process>Enter the following:
• Hiring Process Name
• Description
• Associated Job Type, Department, or any other field.

Step 3: Define Workflow Sub-Stages

By default, the system will show you 4 Stages i.e., New, In-Review, Interview, and Offer. Admin can create a sub-status in these 4 stages.

Click on the “+ Add step” icon to add a sub-status.

Admin can pick a sub-status from the suggested status or click on “+” icon in Add Custom block to create a custom sub-status and enter the sub-status name.

In the same way, you can add more sub-statuses, and once all required sub-statuses are defined, you can click on the “6 dots” icon against each status to define the mandatory and non-mandatory status.

Admin can also connect a workflow for each sub status, along with a notification recipient and assessment integration with a third party.

  • Notifications: Admin can select the role to trigger the notification.

 

  • Workflow: Admin can drag and drop steps to configure the workflow.

 

  • Assessments: Admin can select the assessment vendor, if any.

Best practice:
Keep stages limited to 5–7 steps for a streamlined hiring experience.

Final Thoughts

Designing an efficient hiring process in SmartRecruiters helps organizations create a structured, scalable, and collaborative recruitment workflow.

By properly configuring stages, interview steps, scorecards, and automation, companies can:
• Improve hiring speed
• Ensure consistent candidate evaluation
• Enhance recruiter and hiring manager collaboration

A well-designed hiring workflow ultimately leads to better hiring decisions and improved candidate experience.

entry.title: Stay Current with SAP Cloud ALM: A Look at Newly Released Roadmap Features
entry.content:
Stay Current with SAP Cloud ALM: A Look at Newly Released Roadmap Features

Structure of this blog

In this blog, I highlighted the 10 key functionalities introduced in Cloud ALM in Q1 2026 and Q4 2025.

  • SAP Cloud ALM memory extension
  • New RFC metrics available in Health Monitoring
  • Compliance status in Configuration & Security Analysis - Data Stores
  • Programs for project grouping
  • New library element creation menu in Libraries
  • Advanced checklists in Q-gates
  • Evidence in Test Management
  • Retrofit Functionality
  • Transport owner in Transports section
  • Document relation to test cases
  • What's coming up?
  1. SAP Cloud ALM, memory extension

Usage Rights include SAP Cloud Service subscriptions containing Enterprise Support, cloud editions, in SAP Enterprise Support and in Product Support for Large Enterprises. This included a baseline of 8 GB SAP HANA Memory which has been increased to 24GB

2.New RFC metrics available in Health Monitoring

Monitoring of RFC metrics is now available with the installation of ST-PI 740 Support Package 33 (SP33).

3.Compliance status in Configuration & Security Analysis - Data Stores

The Check Results and the Compliance score of the service or system are now included in the overview cards in the Configuration & Security Analysis - Data Stores app. The data is fetched from the Validation app.

This gives a summary of the compliance status of the service or system in the Overview page of the Data Stores app.

4.Programs for project grouping

We can now group several projects in a Program which can be navigated directly to the assigned projects through links.

5.New library element creation menu in Libraries

The menu for library element creation was remodeled.

6.Upload of development and interface elements via spreadsheet in Libraries

We can now upload Development and Interface elements using the spreadsheet upload functionality. Two new templates are now available to assist with the upload.

7.Q-Gates Advancement

  • Advanced checklists in Q-gates

In dynamic quality gates we  can now choose between standard and advanced checklists quality gates to ensure that project meets specific quality criteria before moving on to the next phase.

The following roadmaps  are supported:

Roadmaps

  • SAP Cloud ERP Private - New Implementation
  • SAP Cloud ERP Private - System Conversion
  • SAP Cloud ERP Private - Selective Data Transition
  • RISE with SAP Methodology - clean core success plan

 

  • View and edit all existing checklist item attributes in Q-gates checklists

We can now view and edit all existing checklist item attributes in the hierarchical view of checklists coming from dynamic quality gates. The following columns have been added:

  • Responsible
  • Planned Completion
  • Team
  • User Type
  • Comments

 

8.Evidence in Test Management

When preparing a manual test case, we can now specify which actions require the testers to provide a screenshot as evidence during the test execution. Test evidence ensures that there's a clear record of what was tested and of the actual result of the executed test action.

Once the evidence is marked as mandatory in test plan as above, it is required to provide screenshots of the actual result as evidence to proceed further. If the test evidence is not added you get below error.

9.Feature Updates

  • Retrofit Functionality and transport owner in Transports section

With the Retrofit app, we can synchronize changed objects from a maintenance development system to project a development system in dual track landscapes. Currently, this is only possible for CTS-managed landscapes. Retrofit between different SAP S/4HANA releases and from ECC to SAP S/4HANA aren't supported.

  • Transport owner in Transports section

We can now see the transport owner in the Transports section.

10.Document relation to test cases

We can now assign or unassign relations to test cases from within the Documents app.

What's coming up?

Ability to lock the process hierarchy

  • To prevent changes of hierarchy trees in SAP Cloud ALM that are received from external systems

Ability to maintain tags on solution activities

  • Speed up the process of finding solution activities when modeling processes in process authoring

Ability to upload document files to SAP Document Management service that runs on SAP BTP platform

Move document files from SAP Solution Manager to the SAP Cloud ALM solution as part of selective data transfer, including:

  • Creating internal documents in SAP Cloud ALM
  • Downloading document files from the content server used by SAP Solution Manager
  • Uploading a document as an external file to SAP Document Management service connected to SAP Cloud ALM

Dashboard for RISE with SAP Methodology: executive view

  • Provide a high-level summary to allow executives quickly access a concise overview of their clean core compliance

 Additional status for 4-tier landscape

  • A new status value to support 4-tier landscapes properly
  • To indicate the deployment readiness for the preproduction system

 Automatic check on transport release

  • Enable an automatic check to be triggered in the ABAP test cockpit tool at Change and Transport System–managed transport release in a feature
  • Prevent transport from being released if the check fails

 Retrofit for upgrade and conversion in SAP S/4HANA

 Extend retrofit to support the following scenarios of ABAP dual landscape synchronization:

  • Upgrade: system tracks on different support packages or software release versions
  • Conversion: SAP ERP Central Component to SAP S/4HANA

I hope this blog helps you find all the required details on what’s new and upcoming on Cloud ALM.

entry.title: Self‑Invoice in Sap Public Cloud: XML for reverse charge and EU/non‑EU services?
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

We are working with SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud for a customer in Italy, and we are facing questions about how to handle self‑invoice (autofattura) in several reverse charge scenarios, especially considering the requirements of Italian e‑invoicing (FatturaPA).

The customer needs to cover three cases:

  • Domestic reverse charge (self‑invoice for local reverse charge).

  • Intra‑EU services.

  • Extra‑EU services.

SAP provides the standard “Print Self‑Invoice” app, but this only allows downloading a PDF. The customer believes that, according to Italian regulations, the self‑invoice should also be generated in structured XML format, and that a PDF alone may not be legally sufficient. We are not fully certain whether the PDF is acceptable in all scenarios, but the customer insists on having XML output.

The challenge is that:

  • SAP Public Cloud is designed to generate XML for outgoing invoices, meaning invoices issued to customers.

  • In a self‑invoice process, the XML would need to be generated for a supplier, not a customer.

  • We cannot find any standard functionality in Public Cloud that generates supplier‑side XML for these self‑invoice scenarios.

  • It is also unclear how these cases could be integrated into the standard Electronic Document Processing / Document Compliance framework, which is focused on outgoing invoicing.

We would like to understand:

  • How are you handling XML generation for self‑invoices in SAP Public Cloud for Italian customers?

  • Are you relying only on the PDF from “Print Self‑Invoice”?

entry.title: Efficient Delta Creation in the Datasphere: A Practical Guide
entry.content:
1                Introduction: The Importance of Delta Processing in Modern Data Architectures
Nowadays, large amounts of data are transported from A to B. Therefore, it is becoming increasingly important to either consume or analyze data virtually, or to consume a delta. Since this is not always possible, this article will show how I have solved this problem within the Datasphere.

Step 1: Extracting Data into a Staging Table
The first step is straightforward: extract the data from the source system into a staging table.

At this stage, the goal is not to transform the data but simply to create a 1:1 copy of the source dataset via Replication Flow. This staging layer represents the most recent snapshdot of the source system. 

The staging table acts as the input layer for delta processing. Each load refreshes this table with the latest data extracted from the source.

Step 2: Creating a Hash for Change Detection
To detect changes efficiently, we generate a hash value for each record based on the relevant business attributes.

The idea behind this is simple: If any field changes, the hash value changes as well.

This avoids expensive field-by-field comparisons and keeps the comparison logic compact.

Example for SQL-View for new data:

SELECT
    MATERIAL,
    MATERIAL_DESC,
    QUANTITY,
    HASH_SHA256(
        TO_BINARY(
            MATERIAL || MATERIAL_DESC || QUANTITY
        )
    ) AS HASH_VALUE,
    LOAD_TS
FROM IL_LT_MATERIAL

This hash essentially acts as a fingerprint of the record. When we compare two datasets later, we only need to compare the hash values instead of every column.

For large datasets, this approach can significantly simplify the change detection process.

Beside the new replicated Data we have the "active" Data in another table, which are loaded before. The hash-value is created as well, but over an Transformation Flow. After that we can compare each line over the key (Material).

Step 3: Calculating the Delta
Once the staging data contains the hash values, it can be compared to the current active dataset.

This comparison identifies three possible scenarios:

  • INSERT – the record exists in staging but not in the active dataset
  • UPDATE – the record exists in both datasets but the hash value changed
  • DELETE – the record exists in the active dataset but not in the new staging data

The first time the pipeline runs (initial load), there is no previous dataset to compare against. In that case, the active dataset is simply initialized.

From the second run onward, the system starts identifying real deltas.

Example logic for SQL-View:

SELECT
N.MATERIAL,
N.MATERIAL_DESC,
N.QUANTITY,
CURRENT_TIMESTAMP AS CHANGE_TS,
CASE
WHEN A.MATERIAL IS NULL THEN 'I'
WHEN N.HASH_VALUE <> A.HASH_VALUE THEN 'U'
WHEN N.MATERIAL IS NULL THEN 'DELETE'
END AS DELTA_TYPE
FROM IL_SV_MATERIAL_HASH N
FULL OUTER JOIN IT_LT_MATERIAL_ACTIVE A
ON S.MATERIAL = A.MATERIAL;

Each detected change is stored together with a timestamp, which allows us to track when the change was detected.

Material_DeltaTable

This timestamp becomes particularly useful for auditing, monitoring, and time-based queries.

Step 4: Maintaining the Delta Table
The detected changes are written into a delta table. Instead of storing the full dataset again, this table only contains the records that changed.

Typical columns include:

  • Business key (e.g. MATERIAL, PLANT)
  • Updated values
  • Change type (INSERT / UPDATE / DELETE)
  • Change timestamp

With each new load, additional delta records are appended.

Over time, the table becomes a history of detected changes, which can be extremely helpful for troubleshooting or reconstructing data states.

Step 5: Providing the Delta to Data Consumers
Once the delta table is maintained, it can be exposed to downstream consumers.

Via Replication Flow the Data can be provided to the target, for e.g. ADLS (Premium Outbound neccessary)

Applications, dashboards, or integration services can query the delta table and filter by change type.

For example:

  • retrieve only new records
  • process updates
  • synchronize deletions

Because only the changed data is provided, consumers do not need to process the entire dataset each time.

This keeps integrations lightweight and improves overall system performance.

Process Overview
The complete workflow can be summarized in five steps:

  1. Extract the latest data into a staging table
  2. Generate a hash value for each record
  3. Compare the staging dataset with the active dataset
  4. Identify inserts, updates, and deletes
  5. Store and expose the delta for downstream consumption

entry.title: Performance Optimization in SAP Composable Storefront
entry.content:
Part One — Stop Fetching Too Much

This is where most of the wins are.

Cache pages that look the same for everyone

The product pages for instance which gets rendered for un authenticated user are the same for every visit to the page and for any un authenticated user . Making a server call each time in such a scenario is a expensive process the solution cache them and prevent a network call.

The only thing to be noted here is that the pages being cached should never have user data or any real time data to be displayed , the cached pages should never persist any data either as this may cause discrepancy.

Easiest thumb rule to keep in mind here is to strip off the authentication headers here so as to ensure there is no authenticated or user data being retrieved even during the initial fetch

Stop sending data nobody asked for

Spartacus when making a call to commerce will get a default set of parameters in the API response. The question is , is everything utilised? Are all these parameters needed in reality. If not we can always customise what is needed the url param and get back only what is needed making the network calls lighter and quicker. 

Similarly on various orientations the slots needed may be lesser than the desktop version using layout.module.ts  optimise the layout and the slots to be rendered this will also reduce the burden of the pages API response and make the app respond quicker.

One API call, no matter how many components need the data

In Spartacus the same data from an API response may be used across pages and components, as long as the data is needed afresh making the same call again and again is an expensive process.

Ngrx is to be leveraged here , and using appropriate selectors the data is to be consumed from the store.

In case we need fresh data the loaded flag corresponding the required data in the store is to be made false so that retrieval of data happens with a fresh API call and subsequent components receive the data update via observable streams

Part Two — Make Angular Work Less

Once the API layer is clean, look at how Angular renders the data it receives.

Tell Angular when not to bother re-checking

By default angular checks every component for any event happening like a click , or a key press or a mouse movement it triggers the entire component tree to be checked as the document tree structure grows and as it becomes data heavy this turns out to be sluggish the obvious reason being the change detection needs to run only in the component where the change has occurred.

We can alter this by marking the component to work with a altered change detection approach called "On push"

This ensures only when a input property of that component changes will the change detection run.

For Spartacus components that read from the store, this means Angular only re-renders them when the store data updates. Everything else is ignored.

Tell Angular how to recognise items in a list

 

Angular is a data driven framework and one of the most commonly used concepts is ngFor where in it iterates over the array and renders the list.

This rendering happens the moment any change happens to the array.

But the question now is if just one of the array item changes rendering the entire DOM tree again is an expensive process. So we need to identify which element of node of the tree has changed this is done using trackBy where in each element or node of tree is identified by a unique identifier.

With this unique identifier only the changed data or node is rendered making it quicker and more efficient also less resource intensive,

Do not load everything upfront

Any site which is loaded is composed of bundles of modules. Now during startup we do not need all modules , for instance a user coming newly to the site needs to complete registration first and wont go to the checkout page right away .

So is it necessary to have the module for the same loaded? No it just makes the bundle heavy and loading process time consuming. This is what happens by default and its a process called eager loading.

We only need to load the modules as and when needed when the user navigates to the required modules. This process is called lazy loading and this needs to be adopted mandatory.

Part Three — Make it Feel Fast

Even a genuinely fast page can feel slow if it shows the user nothing while loading. This section is about perception.

Show the shape of the page before the content arrives

 

When the API is loading, a blank white page feels like something broke. Even a fast response has a delay.

Skeleton screens fix this. Instead of showing nothing, you show rough placeholder shapes where the content will appear — grey blocks in the size of cards, text, images. Usually with a soft shimmer effect.

The user sees the page "filling in" rather than appearing from nowhere. When the real data arrives, it just swaps in. No jarring blank-to-content jump.

Best part — it's pure CSS. No extra JavaScript, no extra API calls. It shows up instantly because it's already in the HTML the server sent.

Performance is just a habit. Teams that build it in from the start compound their gains over time. Teams that leave it for the end spend the whole project paying the cost.

entry.title: ABAP Performance Tuning in S/4HANA Series : Part 1 : Performance Tools
entry.content:
Stop Guessing, Start Profiling: Choosing the Right ABAP Performance Tool

Series: ABAP Performance Tuning in S/4HANA | Part 1 of 7 | Author: Manish Khanna

Your batch job missed its 3-hour window. Your Fiori app takes 12 seconds to open a purchase order. You open SE38, stare at the program, and start adding WRITE statements. This is the wrong approach, and it's costing your project days.

ABAP has a mature performance toolset. The problem is that most developers reach for the wrong tool first — or skip tooling entirely and guess. This series fixes that. Seven posts, each focused on one area, all grounded in real patterns from S/4HANA landscapes.

The Core Problem: Wrong Tool = Wasted Time

Every ABAP performance problem lives in one of three layers:

  1. Database — slow SQL, missing indexes, N+1 SELECTs
  2. ABAP runtime — CPU-heavy loops, string operations, object instantiation
  3. Memory — heap growth, object proliferation, static cache accumulation

There are also S/4HANA-specific layers:

  1. CDS / HANA — CDS view stacking, missing pushdown, unoptimized aggregations
  2. Code quality — anti-patterns catchable before you even run the program

The fastest fix is always in layer 1. A SELECT inside a loop touching 10,000 rows is worth 30 minutes of your time. A 2% ABAP CPU saving is not. Start at the database.

The Tool Landscape

 

Tool Transaction Primary Focus When to Use
ATCATC / ADTStatic code analysisPre-transport — catch anti-patterns before tracing
ST05ST05Database SQLSlow SQL, missing indexes, N+1 SELECT patterns
SATSATABAP CPU runtimeSlow logic, loop inefficiencies, method call overhead
ST12ST12ABAP + MemoryMemory leaks, object proliferation, OO-heavy programs
SM50SM50Live work processesProcess stuck right now, need immediate call stack
/SDF/SMON/SDF/SMONHistorical analysisIssue happened yesterday — need post-mortem call stack
SQL AnalyzerBAS / VS CodeHANA HEX queriesHANA execution plan, Why Not HEX analysis
DBACOCKPITDBACOCKPITDB monitoringHANA cache hit rates, expensive SQL history

Symptom-to-Tool Map

Use this before you do anything else. Match your symptom to the right tool.

 

Symptom Start HereThen
Slow report, unknown causeST05SAT
Long DB wait time in traceST05—
High CPU, low DB timeSATST12 if OO-heavy
Memory dump / STORAGE errorST12SAT
SELECT inside loop suspectedST05—
Fiori timeout / OData 504ST05 on OData backendCDS analysis
CDS view slowST05 + SQL AnalyzerEntity buffer review
Work process stuck (happening now)SM50 live call stackServer snapshot
Batch job was slow (yesterday)/SDF/SMON historical—
HANA aggregation query slowSQL Analyzer (HEX)Result cache
Pre-transport quality checkATC—

The Recommended Workflow

Follow this sequence for every performance engagement. Don't skip steps.

Step 0 — ATC: Run static analysis before any tracing. ATC catches SELECT *, SELECT in loop, missing FAE guards, and COMMIT WORK in loop in seconds. Fix these first — no trace needed.

Step 1 — ST05: Eliminate database problems. This is your highest ROI step. One missing index can cut runtime by 80%.

Step 2 — SAT: Profile ABAP runtime once SQL is clean. Find CPU hotspots, excessive loop iterations, method call overhead.

Step 3 — ST12: Add memory analysis if the program uses ABAP Objects extensively, or if you're seeing memory dumps.

Step 4 — CDS / HANA tuning: For S/4HANA workloads — entity buffers, result caches, HEX analysis.

⚠️Warning: Never run traces in production without authorization. Traces add overhead and may capture sensitive data. Use development or quality systems with representative data volumes — always.

 

 

What This Series Covers

 

Post Topic
Part 1 (this post)Tool selection: which tool for which symptom
Part 2ST05: killing N+1 queries and full table scans
Part 3SAT + ST12: CPU profiling and memory leak detection
Part 4ATC: pre-transport quality gate and CI/CD integration
Part 5S/4HANA: CDS pushdown, entity buffers, result caches
Part 6Production incidents: call stacks, /SDF/SMON, parallel batch
Part 7Complete anti-pattern catalogue (DB · ABAP · OO · CDS · HANA)

Start from the top or jump to the symptom you're facing right now. Each post stands alone.

Key Takeaways

  • Match your symptom to the right tool before you open any code
  • ST05 first — database problems have the highest ROI fix
  • ATC before any trace — it catches the most common anti-patterns for free
  • Never trace in production; never guess without a tool

Next in this series: The ST05 Playbook: Killing N+1 Queries and Full Table Scans in ABAP — the step-by-step guide to SQL trace analysis with real code fixes.

Series Home Page

entry.title: IBP Cloud with multiple ERP using Real-Time Integration
entry.content:
• Overview
  • Target Audience
  • Business Scenarios
  • Scenario 1:Adding an Additional ERP to Existing Planning Area
  • Scenario 2: Additional Planning Area in IBP with one or multiple ERP systems
  • Core Concepts and Terminology
  • Configuration Guide by Scenario
  • Scenario 1: Adding an Additional ERP to Existing Planning Area
  • IBP Inbound Configuration
  • IBP Outbound Configuration
  • Scenario 2: Creating a New IBP Planning Area with Dedicated ERP Data Source(s)
  • IBP Inbound Configuration
  • IBP Outbound Configuration
  • Queue Prefix Strategy
  • Conclusion

Overview

SAP IBP Real-Time Integration (RTI) enables near-real-time, bidirectional replication of data between one or more ERP systems and IBP Cloud. This capability is essential for organizations requiring synchronized master data, transactional data, and planning parameters across distributed enterprise planning landscapes.

While initial Real-Time integration setup follows a well-defined procedure with established options—such as the CIAS guided workflow (strongly recommended for first-time implementations) or documented screenshot guides—this article focuses on extending existing RTI infrastructures with additional ERP systems or IBP planning areas.

 

Target Audience

This article is intended for:

  • Technical Project Leads overseeing landscape extensions and data synchronization strategies
  • IBP Integration Architects responsible for designing multi-system landscapes
  • IBP Consultants implementing and maintaining real-time integration solutions
  • SAP BASIS/ERP Administrators managing on-premise connectivity infrastructure

 

Business Scenarios

This guide addresses two primary scenarios requiring careful configuration:

Scenario 1: Adding an Additional ERP to Existing Planning Area

  • Existing Planning Area, RTI Profile and ERP connectivity
  • New ERP system to be connected
  • Requirement for bidirectional integration (inbound and outbound)

Key Consideration: data from additional ERP system can be integrated into the same Planning Area only if the CIF framework is configured with the same CIF LOGSYS name. This ensures data consolidation from multiple source systems into a single planning context.

Scenario 2: Additional Planning Area in IBP with one or multiple ERP systems

Prerequisites:

  • Existing Planning Area, RTI Profile and ERP connectivity
  • One or more new ERP systems 
  • Requirement for isolated or specialized planning logic

Key Consideration: A new, uniquely-named CIF LOGSYS must be created, distinct from existing identifiers, to establish a separate integration route.

In both cases there are similar steps that have to be performed, however there are slight differences as well that I will detail out in the next sections.

 

Core Concepts and Terminology

Before addressing configuration, understanding the following key concepts is essential:

CIF Logsys
The unique (10 chars long) identifier that defines an integration route from an on-premise ERP system to an IBP planning Area. We usually use it in the following transactions: BD54, NDV2, CFC1, CFC91, SM59, CFI1

Real-Time Integration Profile
A configuration object that establishes the connection between:

  • A specific IBP Planning Area (data target)
  • A specific CIF LOGSYS value (integration route). In the Real-Time integration profile app this field is called "IBP Logical System".
  • Mapping and transformation rules for incoming data
  • Settings for outgoing data

Critical Rule: A CIF LOGSYS value uniquely determines the destination Planning Area. Incoming RTI messages contain the CIF LOGSYS identifier, which the system uses to look up the corresponding RTI Profile and route data accordingly.

Planning Area
The IBP object serving as the final data repository, encompassing:

  • Master data (materials, locations, suppliers)
  • Key figures (demand, supply, inventory)
  • Planning levels and hierarchies
  • Time profiles and calendars

 

Configuration Guide by Scenario

Scenario 1: Adding an Additional ERP to Existing Planning Area

Pre-Configuration Checklist

  • Determine the target Planning Area
  • Check the linked RTI Profile and note the CIF LOGSYS, this will be needed in the configuration steps

 

IBP Inbound Configuration

Step 1: Communication Arrangements (IBP)
  • SAP_COM_0805 - No action required. This is a technical communication setup between IBP Cloud and BTP which can be reused without modification for this scenario.
  • SAP_COM_0769  - used for transactional data integration from ERP to IBP.  It must be extended with the new logsys of the new ERP system. 
  • SAP_COM_0770 - used for master data integration from ERP to IBP. It must be extended with the new logsys of the new ERP system

For logsys determination of the source system and client you can use T000 table in source system. Add that value into the below multi value field:

If you want to have dedicated technical for each ERP -> IBP connection:

  1. Create new Communication User in IBP
  2. assign the new Communication User to a new or existing (used by existing SAP_COM_0769 or 0770) Communication System
  3. Create new Communication Arrangements inherited from SAP_COM_0769 and 0770 and assign the logsys of the source system to it and select the new Communication User for Inbound Communication section
Step 2: Cloud Connector (On-Premise Infrastructure)
  • On-Premise to Cloud settings: No additional configuration required. The existing service channel to IBP Cloud remains active and can handle traffic from multiple ERPs simultaneously.
Step 3: ERP-Side CIF Configuration (in the new ERP)
  • Configure CIF using the CIF LOGSYS you noted down as part of the pre-configuration checklist (BD54 | NDV2 | CFC1 | SM59 | CFC1).
    • Follow chapters 5.3, 5.4, 5.5 in screenshot guide
    • In SM59 use either the existing Inbound Communication user or the new one depending on how you performed the configuration in Step 1.
  • Make sure that connection is working (SM59 authorization test)
Step 4: RTI Profile Update (IBP)

Modify the existing RTI Profile:

  • Inbound Systems section:  Add the new ERP's logsys identifier

(Optional: BADI Enhancement)
  • If the source ERP's data requires transformation or enrichment (e.g., adding prefixes/suffixes to distinguish between source systems):
  • Implement BADI (Business Add-In) in the ERP system, use the BADI to intercept and modify CIF outbound messages before transmission. Common use cases:
    • Prefixing material codes (`ERP1_MAT001`)
    • Filtering sensitive data or consolidating duplicate records

 

IBP Outbound Configuration

Step 1: ERP-Side Technical User Setup
  • Create two dedicated technical users in the new ERP system:
    • Configuration User: technical user used for Initial configuration data pull (Calendar, UoM, Currency)
    • Transactional User: technical user used for planning data integration to ERP
  • Make sure that PFCG roles properly assigned
  • Follow chapters 6.1, 6.2 in screenshot guide
Step 2: Cloud Connector Mapping
  • Create a new Cloud-to-On-Premise mapping specific to the additional ERP
  • assign RFC resources to the new mapping rule
  • Follow chapters 4.2 and 4.4 in screenshot guide. If you have multiple application servers in ERP side (Load Balancing case, use Chapter 4.3 instead of 4.2)
Step 3: Communication Arrangements (IBP)

Create new Communication Arrangements for outbound data delivery:

  • SAP_COM_0574 - No action required. This is a technical communication setup between IBP Cloud and BTP.  in case of landscape extension with additional ERP system, this communication will be reused automatically.
  • SAP_COM_0684 - Create new specific to the target ERP.  Use the cloud connection mapping created in the previous step.
  • SAP_COM_0685 - Create new specific to the target ERP. Use the cloud connection mapping created in the previous step.
  • Follow chapters 6.3 and 6.4 in screenshot guide. 

Step 4: RTI Profile - Update Outbound Systems (IBP)

  • Open the RTI Profile linked to the target Planning Area
  • Add the new ERP's logsys to the Outbound Systems list

Important: If this is the first time the Outbound Systems list contains more than one entry, you must define distribution logic for all outbound logsys entries otherwise existing integrations will not work. 

Step 5: BGRFC Inbound configuration (ERP)

When you setup an ERP system the first time for IBP Real-Time integration, the bgrfc framework has to be prepared

  • bgrfc scheduler configuration
  • Inbound destination for messages that starts with specific queue prefix.
  • Follow chapters 6.5 in screenshot guide. 

 

Scenario 2: Creating a New IBP Planning Area with Dedicated ERP Data Source(s)

Pre-Configuration Checklist

  • Determine the new Planning Area (master data, key figures, hierarchies)
  • New RTI Profile should be created to the new Planning Area
  • Define a new CIF LOGSYS which was never used before

IBP Inbound Configuration

Step 1: Communication Arrangements (IBP)
  • SAP_COM_0805 - No action required. This is a technical communication setup between IBP Cloud and BTP.  in case of landscape extension with additional ERP system, this communication will be reused automatically.
  • SAP_COM_0769  - used for master data integration from ERP to IBP.  It must be extended with the new logsys of the new ERP system
  • SAP_COM_0770 - used for transactional data integration from ERP to IBP. It must be extended with the new logsys of the new ERP system

If you want to have dedicated technical for each ERP -> IBP connection:

  1. Create new Communication User in IBP
  2. assign the new Communication User to a new or existing (used by existing SAP_COM_0769 or 0770) Communication System
  3. Create new Communication Arrangements inherited from SAP_COM_0769 and 0770 and assign the logsys of the source system to it and select the new Communication User for Inbound Communication section
Step 2: Cloud Connector
  • On-Premise to Cloud settings: No additional configuration required. The existing service channel to IBP Cloud remains active and can handle traffic from multiple ERPs simultaneously.
Step 3: ERP-Side Configuration
  • Configure CIF using the CIF LOGSYS you noted down as part of the pre-configuration checklist (BD54 | NDV2 | CFC1 | SM59 | CFC1).
    • Follow chapters 5.3, 5.4, 5.5 in screenshot guide
    • In SM59 use either the existing Inbound Communication user or the new one depending on how you performed the configuration in Step 1.
  • Make sure that connection is working (SM59 authorization test
Step 4: IBP Configuration

Create new objects:

  • New Planning Area:
    • Define master data dimensions (materials, locations, suppliers, etc.)
    • Configure key figures (forecasts, safety stock, demand patterns)
    • Establish planning levels and aggregation hierarchies
    • Set time profiles (daily, weekly, monthly planning buckets)
  • New RTI Profile:
    • IBP logsys: must match the value of the new CIF LOGSYS created in Step 3
    • Inbound Systems: Add the source ERP's LOGSYS
    • Mapping rules: Define field-to-field transformations from ERP to IBP

 

IBP Outbound Configuration

Step 1: ERP-Side Technical Users
  • Create two dedicated technical users in the new ERP system:
    • Configuration User: technical user used for Initial configuration data pull (Calendar, UoM, Currency)
    • Transactional User: technical user used for planning data integration to ERP
  • Make sure that PFCG roles properly assigned
  • Follow chapters 6.1, 6.2 in screenshot guide
Step 2: Cloud Connector Mapping
  • Create a new Cloud-to-On-Premise mapping specific to the additional ERP
  • assign RFC resources to the new mapping rule
  • Follow chapters 4.2 and 4.4 in screenshot guide. If you have multiple application servers in ERP side (Load Balancing case, use Chapter 4.3 instead of 4.2)
Step 3: Communication Arrangements (IBP)

Create new Communication Arrangements for outbound data delivery:

  • SAP_COM_0574 - No action required. This is a technical communication setup between IBP Cloud and BTP.  in case of landscape extension with additional ERP system, this communication will be reused automatically.
  • SAP_COM_0684 - Create new specific to the target ERP.  Use the cloud connection mapping created in the previous step.
  • SAP_COM_0685 - Create new specific to the target ERP. Use the cloud connection mapping created in the previous step.
  • Follow chapters 6.3 and 6.4 in screenshot guide. 
Step 4: RTI Profile - Update Outbound Systems (IBP)
  • Open the RTI Profile linked to the target Planning Area
  • Add the new ERP's logsys to the Outbound Systems list

Important: If this is the first time the Outbound Systems list contains more than one entry, you must define distribution logic for all outbound logsys entries otherwise IBP outbound integration will not work. 

Step 5: BGRFC Inbound configuration (ERP)

When you setup an ERP system the first time for IBP Real-Time integration, the bgrfc framework has to be prepared

  • bgrfc scheduler configuration
  • Inbound destination for messages that starts with specific queue prefix.
  • Follow chapters 6.5 in screenshot guide. 

Queue Prefix Strategy

If you integrate from multiple ERP systems to an IBP Cloud system it is useful to separate the queues by CIF LOGSYS. It has the following benefits:

  • Enables queue-level monitoring and alerting
  • Simplifies debugging by isolating traffic for each integration route

Conclusion

Extending SAP IBP landscape to support multiple ERP systems with Real-Time integration capabilities requires methodical planning and precise configuration. The distinction between consolidating data into a single Planning Area (Scenario 1) versus creating specialized Planning Areas with separate integration routes (Scenario 2) fundamentally determines the CIF LOGSYS naming, RTI Profile structure, and distribution logic.

By adhering to these guidelines, organizations can build scalable, maintainable multi-system planning landscapes that deliver real-time data synchronization and support complex business processes across distributed enterprises.

entry.title: Orchestrating AI Assisted HR Workflows with Microsoft Copilot Using SAP BTP and SuccessFactors
entry.content:
Employees increasingly expect quick answers to questions such as:

  • What is the status of my promotion request

  • Which onboarding tasks are still pending for new hires

The objective was to allow AI-assisted HR queries while maintaining strict governance over SAP SuccessFactors data and workflows.

The organization had very clear requirements. AI tools should not have direct access to SuccessFactors APIs, and they should not execute actions using external tokens. All interactions had to remain fully controlled, auditable, and aligned with HR governance policies.

To achieve this, we designed an architecture where SAP BTP served as the orchestration and control layer between Microsoft Copilot and SuccessFactors.

When a user interacts with Copilot and asks a question related to HR processes, the request is not sent directly to SuccessFactors. Instead, it is routed to SAP Integration Suite running on SAP BTP.

At this stage, the integration layer performs several important functions. The request is validated, enriched with user context, and evaluated against access policies. This ensures that the request complies with the organization’s HR security and authorization rules.

Once validated, the request triggers a workflow through SAP Build Process Automation. This workflow layer applies business rules and approval logic related to HR processes. For example, certain HR data may only be accessible to managers or HR partners, and some actions may require additional validation steps.

Only after these governance checks are completed does the integration layer interact with SAP SuccessFactors modules such as Employee Central, onboarding workflows, or performance management services.

The requested information is retrieved in a controlled manner and transformed into a simplified response that can be returned to the AI interface.

From the user’s perspective, Copilot appears to instantly provide HR insights. However, behind the scenes the request has passed through a fully governed integration and workflow orchestration layer within SAP BTP.

This approach provided several advantages for the organization. AI could enhance the employee experience without introducing uncontrolled access to HR systems. All interactions remained traceable and compliant with internal policies. At the same time, the architecture created a scalable foundation for future AI-driven HR scenarios.

As enterprises continue integrating AI assistants into workplace tools, the key challenge will not be enabling AI itself but ensuring it operates within controlled enterprise architectures. Platforms such as SAP BTP provide the necessary capabilities to bridge modern AI services with critical business applications while maintaining governance, security, and operational visibility.

entry.title: Rebase, Merge, Repeat: The Power of the Parallel Line
entry.content:
Co-Author: Disha

In today’s fast-paced business landscape, transformation doesn’t happen in a single wave. It happens in phases, across countries, business units, and multiple innovation cycles. SAP recognized this – and introduced the parallel line in SAP Central Business Configuration.

The parallel line is more than a second line. It’s a strategic enabler for structured, scalable, and low-risk digital transformation in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

What Is the Parallel Line?

The parallel line allows you to run multiple configuration tracks in parallel within SAP Central Business Configuration – without disrupting the main implementation line.

Instead of being limited to a single linear workspace, you can now:

  • Run country rollouts in parallel
  • Extend scope and introduce new business processes after go-live without disrupting daily operations
  • Innovate safely while production remains stable

It brings enterprise-grade flexibility into SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Switching to the parallel line

Why Parallel Line Is a Game Changer

The parallel line turns SAP Central Business Configuration into a transformation platform – not just a configuration tool. In the following sections, you’ll learn why.

Enables Global Rollouts with Confidence

Are you planning to roll out to a new country? Launch a new business unit? Introduce additional processes?

Large organizations rarely go live in all countries at once. With the parallel line, if your organization is rolling out operations to new legal entities or regions, you can extend scope and configure country-specific processes in a separate line.

  • A global template can go live in one country
  • Additional countries can be configured in parallel
  • Each rollout follows a structured governance path

Safe Space for Transformation

The parallel line allows you to create a copy of your existing workspace and carry out large or complex business configuration changes independently from your main line:

  • Introduce new modules over time
  • Expand functional scope step by step
  • Redesign organizational structures
  • Adjust operating models gradually

Your main line stays stable and focused, while innovation happens in parallel.

Test Before You Commit

One of the greatest strengths of the parallel line is the ability to configure and test thoroughly before merging changes into the main line. This structured approach reduces risk and increases implementation quality. Your productive landscape remains stable while you prepare for the next stage of growth.

Stay Aligned with Rebase

Business doesn’t stand still – and neither does your main line.

Once your parallel line is created, it can easily receive updates from the main line through a rebase operation. This keeps both lines synchronized and aligned, ensuring your workspace remains consistent and up to date.

You innovate in parallel, but you stay connected.

Options to rebase and merge the parallel line with the main line

Full Flexibility — Even to Start Over

If your strategy changes, you can delete the parallel line and start fresh (via SAP for Me). That level of flexibility ensures you remain in control throughout your transformation journey.

You’re never locked in – you’re always empowered.

How the Parallel Line and the Configuration Localization Tool Accelerate Localization

Example: Germany, Malta, and Cayman Islands

Imagine a company headquartered in Germany rolling out SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to Malta and the Cayman Islands.

Main Line (Germany HQ)

  • Chart of accounts
  • Core finance processes
  • Intercompany setup

Malta (Parallel Line)

  • 18% Value-Added Tax (VAT) setup and reduced rates
  • EU reverse charge
  • Local VAT returns & reporting formats

Cayman Islands (Parallel Line)

  • Local tax rules and reporting formats
  • Currency setup
  • Compliance with local regulatory requirements

How It Works

  1. The Global Template team configures core finance and procurement processes in the main line.
  2. The Malta and Cayman Islands teams configure local settings using the Configuration Localization Tool at the same time in the parallel line.
  3. Parallel testing ensures integration across countries, validates VAT calculations, and checks statutory reporting.
  4. The go-live can happen simultaneously or in planned waves, with confidence that the global template remains intact.

Key Outcomes

  • Faster rollout: You can configure and test multiple countries/regions in parallel.
  • Protected global template: Local changes for the countries localized with the Configuration Localization Tool don’t overwrite core configuration.
  • Early compliance validation: Intercompany and tax compliance issues can be detected sooner.
  • Scalable multi-country deployment: Ideal for companies expanding globally.
  • Better Resource Utilization: Teams work independently without waiting for others.

The parallel line keeps your core stable while you adapt, innovate, and localize faster. Simple, safe, scalable. Note that this feature is exclusively available in SAP Central Business Configuration with the SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, 3-system landscape.

Did you like this blog? If so, please leave us a thumbs up at the top. Also, make sure to follow the SAP Central Business Configuration tag or follow this dedicated blog collection to stay up to date with the latest blog posts.

Do you have any ideas on how to further enhance SAP Central Business Configuration? Go to the Customer Influence platform to submit an improvement request or vote for the ideas of others. This helps us understand your requirements even better and prioritize our work accordingly. We’re looking forward to your improvement requests.

entry.title: Quotations in Supply Protection: Closing the Gap in Advanced ATP
entry.content:
With SAP S/4HANA 2025 FPS1, we're introducing support for quotations as an additional document type in Supply Protection (SUP). This enhancement addresses a critical gap in the Advanced Available-to-Promise (aATP) process and brings more consistency to how we handle different sales document types.

The Business Challenge

Previously, quotations operated outside the Supply Protection framework, creating a potential bypass that could lead to two significant issues:

  • Customer disappointment
    Imagine confirming a quotation without Supply Protection restrictions, only to have the subsequent call-off blocked because protection has been consumed elsewhere. 
  • Blocked ATP quantities
    Reserved quantities from confirmed quotations lock up inventory that no one can access, reducing overall supply chain flexibility.

By including quotations in Supply Protection, we can now optimize restriction and protection already at the quotation stage, ensuring more reliable commitments and better inventory utilization.

How It Works

The activation itself is straightforward, based on the characteristics catalog for sales documents. However, the real sophistication lies in the transfer logic—similar to what we already support for contracts.

  • Consumption and Transfer
    When a quotation matches Supply Protection, it consumes protection according to the confirmed quantity. The subsequent sales order (call-off) then checks against and uses this consumed protection. The system automatically transfers the consumption from the quotation's time bucket to the call-off's time bucket.
  • Handling Deletions
    If you delete the call-off, the quantity transfers back to the quotation. Note that rejections work differently and don't trigger this reversal.

Illustration: The consumption of Supply Protection by a matching quotation and the transfer of the protection with a subsequent order (call-off)

 

The Order Probability Question

Here's where it gets interesting. In standard aATP processing, quotations support an order probability setting (0-100%), which determines what portion of the requested quantity gets reserved. This makes sense—not every quotation converts to an order.

However, in Supply Protection, we ignore the order probability and consume the full confirmed quantity.

Why this approach?

Let's walk through a scenario: A quotation for 100 units matches a time bucket in Supply Protection. If we consume only 70 units (based on 70% probability), the remaining protection of 30 units could be allocated to other demands.
In a second step, a call-off for the full requested quantity is created, and there is a shortage situation.
The result? The customer receives only 70 units, and part of the reservation for the quotation still blocks some inventory.

The same logic applies to Product Allocation (PAL).
Since there's no perfect solution that satisfies all scenarios, we recommend setting order probability to 100% when using Supply Protection with quotations. This aligns with what most customers already practice.

 

Technical Implementation

Quotations behave consistently with other protected document types, following the same matching and consumption rules you're already familiar with from Supply Protection.

The transfer logic ensures that protection consumption moves seamlessly from quotation to call-off, maintaining data integrity throughout the sales process while preventing double-consumption or orphaned reservations.

This enhancement represents another step toward comprehensive supply chain control in aATP. By closing the quotation gap, we're helping you make more reliable commitments to customers while maintaining better control over your available inventory.

 

Related Information

SAP Help Documentation:
- Best Practice: How to Use Supply Protection in Quotations Effectively
- Supported Document-Type: Quotation

Community Blogs:
- Supply Protection and Fashion Quantity Contracts
- Supply Protection in a Nutshell
- Supply Protection and Other Methods in aATP
