entry.title: How to create a RAP application using a parameterized CDS view
entry.content:
Hello everyone, recently I came across a somewhat unusual requirement where I needed to use a parameter in a RAP application. As we know, this isn’t natively supported with Fiori Elements, so I had to look for a way to make it work. After some research and a few tests, I ended up with a solution that I consider quite innovative: using a Fiori Freestyle approach.

The best part is that it requires only a minimal amount of code, something that any ABAP developer can implement without much effort, and it becomes extremely useful whenever you need to work with parameters that are not directly linked to the data exposed by the CDS view.

The idea behind this solution is simple:

Since projection views in RAP do not support CDS parameters directly, we expose a parameterized interface CDS view through a service definition and service binding.

Then, using a Fiori Freestyle application with a Smart Table, we dynamically inject the parameter into the table binding path before the data request is sent to the backend.

This allows the RAP service to consume the parameterized CDS view without requiring complex workarounds.

 

Let’s go through the required steps:

 

1)  Since it’s not possible to add WITH PARAMETERS to a projection view, we need to create an interface CDS view and use it as our “projection.”

@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: '<your cds description>'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: false
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true

define view entity <your_parameterized_interface_cds>
  with parameters
    p_date : abap.char(8)

as select from <your_source_cds_or_table>
(
  p_date : $parameters.p_date
)
{
    key <your_key_field>,
        <your_field_1>,
        <your_field_2>,
        <your_field_3>
}

2) Create our service definition normally

3) Create Service Binding ( Odata V2 )

4) Now we have to create the freestyle app with the fiori generator application

5) Now just connect to the S4 and select the service binding created earlier

At this point, you can see the project structure in the Explorer panel on the left. We’ll focus only on the view and controller folders.

 

 

 

6)

First, we need to create our Smart Table in the view folder. Inside the SmartFilterBar, we must set the entitySet from our service binding. After that, we add a customControl in my case, I used a DatePicker, but you can use any control that fits your requirement. I also attached an onDateChange event to the DatePicker so we can capture and manipulate the selected date whenever it changes.

Within the Smart Table, we also need to define the same entitySet. Another very important point here is the beforeRebindTable event. This is the hook that allows us to pass the parameter to the CDS view. It will trigger the onBeforeRebindTable method in the controller, where we can dynamically inject the parameter into the binding before the data is requested.

 

<mvc:View
    xmlns:table="sap.ui.table"
    xmlns="sap.ui.comp.smartfilterbar"
    xmlns:f="sap.f"
    xmlns:m="sap.m"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns:smartTable="sap.ui.comp.smarttable"
    controllerName="<your.app.namespace>.controller.Main"
    height="100%">

<f:DynamicPage
    id="dynamicPage"
    fitContent="true"
    title="{i18n>appTitle}">

<f:header>
<f:DynamicPageHeader>

<SmartFilterBar
    id="smartFilterBar"
    entitySet="<your_entity_set>">

    <controlConfiguration>

        <ControlConfiguration
            key="<your_parameter_field>"
            visibleInAdvancedArea="true"
            label="<your_filter_label>"
            mandatory="true">

            <customControl>

                <m:DatePicker
                    id="datePicker"
                    displayFormat="dd/MM/yyyy"
                    valueFormat="yyyyMMdd"
                    placeholder="dd/mm/yyyy"
                    width="100%"
                    required="true"
                    change="onDateChange"/>

            </customControl>

        </ControlConfiguration>

    </controlConfiguration>

</SmartFilterBar>

</f:DynamicPageHeader>
</f:header>

<f:content>

<smartTable:SmartTable
    id="smartTable"
    beforeRebindTable="onBeforeRebindTable"
    showRowCount="true"
    enableAutoBinding="false"
    entitySet="<your_entity_set>"
    smartFilterId="smartFilterBar"
    useVariantManagement="true"
    tableType="Table"
    initiallyVisibleFields="<your_fields>"
    enableAutoColumnWidth="true"
    showTablePersonalisation="true">

    <smartTable:customToolbar>

        <m:OverflowToolbar design="Transparent">
            <m:ToolbarSpacer/>
        </m:OverflowToolbar>

    </smartTable:customToolbar>

</smartTable:SmartTable>

</f:content>

</f:DynamicPage>

</mvc:View>

7) In this step, we implement the logic inside the onBeforeRebindTable method, which is responsible for dynamically passing the parameter to the CDS view before the Smart Table triggers the data request.

First, the code retrieves the value entered in the DatePicker using byId. This value will be used as the parameter for the CDS. A local variable (sDate) is initialized to store the selected date.

Next, a validation is performed to ensure that the date field is filled in. Since this parameter is mandatory for the query, if no value is provided the DatePicker is set to the Error state and a validation message is displayed. At the same time, the table binding is stopped using:

oBindingParams.preventTableBind = true;

This prevents the Smart Table from calling the backend without the required parameter, avoiding unnecessary requests and possible errors.

If the date is correctly filled in, any previous error state is cleared by setting the ValueState back to None.

Finally, the most important part happens: the table binding path is updated dynamically. Here we pass the selected date to the parameterized CDS by changing the binding path of the Smart Table:

setTableBindingPath("/<EntitySet>(p_date='${sDate}')/Set")

This is what allows the RAP application to consume a CDS with parameters, because the parameter is injected at runtime just before the data is requested from the backend.

onBeforeRebindTable: function (oEvent) {

    let oDatePicker = this.byId("datePicker");
    let sDate = "";

    if (oDatePicker) {
        sDate = oDatePicker.getValue();
    }

    if (!sDate) {

        if (oDatePicker) {
            oDatePicker.setValueState("Error");
            oDatePicker.setValueStateText("Please inform the date.");
        }

        let oBindingParams = oEvent.getParameter("bindingParams");

        if (oBindingParams) {
            oBindingParams.preventTableBind = true;
        }

        return;
    }

    if (oDatePicker) {
        oDatePicker.setValueState("None");
    }

    this.getView()
        .byId("smartTable")
        .setTableBindingPath("/<your_entity_set>(p_date='" + sDate + "')/Set");

}

And Vooooalá!

Conclusion

Although RAP and Fiori Elements provide powerful tools for building enterprise applications, there are still scenarios where certain features are not directly supported, such as consuming parameterized CDS views in projection layers.

By combining RAP services with a lightweight Fiori Freestyle approach, we can overcome this limitation in a simple and flexible way. Using events like beforeRebindTable and dynamically modifying the binding path allows parameters to be passed to the CDS view at runtime.

This approach requires only a small amount of additional UI code and can be implemented quickly by ABAP developers who are already familiar with RAP and Fiori.

I hope this solution helps others facing similar challenges when working with parameterized CDS views in RAP applications.

entry.title: Visual Filters in SAP Fiori Elements: A Complete Guide
entry.content:
Introduction :  SAP Fiori Elements provides several smart UI features that help users interact with business data more efficiently. One such feature is the Visual Filter, which offers a graphical way to filter data in applications like the Analytical List Page. Instead of entering filter values manually, users can select values directly from charts or graphical elements, making the filtering process faster and more intuitive. Visual Filters enhance the user experience by combining data visualization with filtering capabilities, allowing users to quickly analyze data and refine their search results.

Visual Filter:

A Visual Filter in SAP Fiori Elements is a graphical filter control used in the Filter Bar of applications like  Analytical List Page.
Instead of typing filter values manually, users can select values directly from a chart (bar, line, or donut) to filter the data.

A Visual Filter is a small interactive chart in the filter bar that allows users to filter data visually.

-----> Visual Filter = Chart + Filter

It shows aggregated data in a small chart, and when the user clicks a value, the application filters the table data automatically.

Example:

Month_YearNet Value

MAY-20231930.7
SEP-2022254.6
FEB-2023245.5

When a user clicks FEB-2023 (245.5) in the Visual Filter, the application automatically filters the data and displays the corresponding results in the chart and list. Please  find the below Screen shot for your Reference.

 

How to Create Visual Filter  in  BTP BAS:

  1. Open SAP Business Application Studio.

  2. Go to the Explorer panel.

  3. Locate your Fiori project (for example: analytical_report).

  4. Right-click on the project.

  5. Select Open Guided Development.

This option helps developers configure Fiori Elements features

After opening Guided Development:

  1. Select Project Guides.

  2. Choose Analytical List Page.

  3. Scroll down in the options.

  4. Click Add a New Visual Filter to the Filter Bar.

This option is used to create a visual filter chart in the filter bar.

After selecting Add a New Visual Filter to the Filter Bar, a configuration wizard will open.

You will see these tabs:

  • About – Information about the visual filter feature

  • Step 1 – Select entity set and dimension

  • Step 2 – Select measure and chart type

  • Step 3 – Generate the annotation and save

Once the configuration is completed, BAS automatically generates the required CDS/UI annotations.

Please find the Below Screen shot for Your Reference:

Step1:

  <Annotation Term="UI.Chart" Qualifier="Step1_CQ">
                    <Record Type="UI.ChartDefinitionType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="ChartType" EnumMember="UI.ChartType/Bar"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Title" String="Purchase Order Data"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Description" String="Purchase Order Data"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Dimensions">
                            <Collection>
                                <PropertyPath>Month_Year</PropertyPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="DimensionAttributes">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="UI.ChartDimensionAttributeType">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Dimension" PropertyPath="Month_Year"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Role" EnumMember="UI.ChartDimensionRoleType/Category"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Measures">
                            <Collection>
                                <PropertyPath>Amount</PropertyPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="MeasureAttributes">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="UI.ChartMeasureAttributeType">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Measure" PropertyPath="Amount"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Role" EnumMember="UI.ChartMeasureRoleType/Axis1"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>

Step2:

 <Annotation Term="UI.PresentationVariant" Qualifier="Step2_pQ">
                    <Record Type="UI.PresentationVariantType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="Visualizations">
                            <Collection>
                                <AnnotationPath>@UI.Chart#Step1_CQ</AnnotationPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>

Step3:

 <Annotation Term="Common.ValueList">
                    <Record Type="Common.ValueListType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="CollectionPath" String="Ztest_ALP_Report"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="PresentationVariantQualifier" String="Step2_pQ"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Parameters">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="Common.ValueListParameterInOut">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="LocalDataProperty" PropertyPath="Month_Year"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="ValueListProperty" String="Month_Year"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>

After completing all the steps, the Visual Filter is successfully added to the Analytical List Page. The filter now appears in the filter bar as an interactive chart, allowing users to select values and automatically filter the data displayed in the charts and table.

 Before  add Visual Filter --- > in the Annotation.xml File.

<edmx:Edmx xmlns:edmx="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edmx" Version="4.0">
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/Common.xml">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="com.sap.vocabularies.Common.v1" Alias="Common"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/UI.xml">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1" Alias="UI"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:Reference Uri="/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZTEST_PURCHASE_DATA_SRV/$metadata">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="ZTEST_PURCHASE_DATA_SRV" Alias="SAP"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:DataServices>
        <Schema xmlns="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edm" Namespace="local">
           
        </Schema>
    </edmx:DataServices>
</edmx:Edmx>

 After  add Visual Filter --- > in the Annotation.xml File.

  <Annotations Target="SAP.Ztest_ALP_ReportType">
                <Annotation Term="UI.Chart" Qualifier="Step1_CQ">
                    <Record Type="UI.ChartDefinitionType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="ChartType" EnumMember="UI.ChartType/Bar"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Title" String="Purchase Order Data"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Description" String="Purchase Order Data"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Dimensions">
                            <Collection>
                                <PropertyPath>Month_Year</PropertyPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="DimensionAttributes">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="UI.ChartDimensionAttributeType">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Dimension" PropertyPath="Month_Year"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Role" EnumMember="UI.ChartDimensionRoleType/Category"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Measures">
                            <Collection>
                                <PropertyPath>Amount</PropertyPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="MeasureAttributes">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="UI.ChartMeasureAttributeType">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Measure" PropertyPath="Amount"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Role" EnumMember="UI.ChartMeasureRoleType/Axis1"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>
                <Annotation Term="UI.PresentationVariant" Qualifier="Step2_pQ">
                    <Record Type="UI.PresentationVariantType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="Visualizations">
                            <Collection>
                                <AnnotationPath>@UI.Chart#Step1_CQ</AnnotationPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>
            </Annotations>
            <Annotations Target="SAP.Ztest_ALP_ReportType/Month_Year">
                <Annotation Term="Common.ValueList">
                    <Record Type="Common.ValueListType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="CollectionPath" String="Ztest_ALP_Report"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="PresentationVariantQualifier" String="Step2_pQ"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Parameters">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="Common.ValueListParameterInOut">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="LocalDataProperty" PropertyPath="Month_Year"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="ValueListProperty" String="Month_Year"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>
            </Annotations>

Note: Instead of following the guided development steps, you can also add the required annotations directly in the annotation file based on the client’s requirements. This approach allows developers to configure Visual Filters manually using CDS/UI annotations.

Onces done  all step sucessfuly you wull see visula filer in the  anaytical list page

Complete  Code --> Visula Filter + Analytical List Page

1.CDS View:
---------------
@AbapCatalog.sqlViewName: 'ZALPR'
@AbapCatalog.compiler.compareFilter: true
@AbapCatalog.preserveKey: true
@AccessControl.authorizationCheck: #NOT_REQUIRED
@EndUserText.label: 'ALP Report'
@Metadata.ignorePropagatedAnnotations: true
@Metadata.allowExtensions: true
define view Ztest_ALP_Report
  as select from ztest_sales_tab
{
  key vbeln as SalesDocument,
      vkorg as SalesOrg,
      werks as Plant,
      vtweg as DistrChannel,
      @DefaultAggregation: #SUM
      netwr as Amount,
      waerk as Currency,
      audat as D_Date,
      case substring( audat, 5, 2 )
      when '01' then concat( 'JAN-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '02' then concat( 'FEB-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '03' then concat( 'MAR-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '04' then concat( 'APR-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '05' then concat( 'MAY-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '06' then concat( 'JUN-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '07' then concat( 'JUL-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '08' then concat( 'AUG-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '09' then concat( 'SEP-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '10' then concat( 'OCT-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '11' then concat( 'NOV-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )
      when '12' then concat( 'DEC-', substring( audat, 1, 4 ) )

      end   as Month_Year
}
2.Meta data extension View:
---------------------------
@Metadata.layer: #CORE

@UI.chart: [{
    qualifier: 'chartDefault',
    description: 'Purchase Data',
    chartType: #PIE,
    dimensions: ['Month_Year'],
    measures: ['Amount'],
    dimensionAttributes: [{ dimension: 'Month_Year',role: #SERIES }],
    measureAttributes: [{ measure: 'Amount', role: #AXIS_1 }]
    }
   ]

@UI.presentationVariant: [{
    qualifier: 'Default',
    visualizations: [{ type: #AS_CHART ,qualifier: 'chartDefault'}]
   
     
}]



annotate entity Ztest_ALP_Report with
{
  .lineItem: [{ position: 10 }]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 10 }]

  SalesDocument;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 20 }]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 20 }]
  SalesOrg;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 30 }]
  Plant;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 40 }]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 40 }]
  DistrChannel;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 50 }]
  .dataPoint.qualifier: 'Amount'
  Amount;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 60 }]
  Currency;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 70 }]
  D_Date;
  .lineItem: [{ position: 80 }]
  .selectionField: [{ position: 80 }]
  Month_Year;

}

3. Annotation.xml File:
-----------------------
<edmx:Edmx xmlns:edmx="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edmx" Version="4.0">
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/Common.xml">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="com.sap.vocabularies.Common.v1" Alias="Common"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:Reference Uri="https://sap.github.io/odata-vocabularies/vocabularies/UI.xml">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="com.sap.vocabularies.UI.v1" Alias="UI"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:Reference Uri="/sap/opu/odata/sap/ZTEST_PURCHASE_DATA_SRV/$metadata">
        <edmx:Include Namespace="ZTEST_PURCHASE_DATA_SRV" Alias="SAP"/>
    </edmx:Reference>
    <edmx:DataServices>
        <Schema xmlns="http://docs.oasis-open.org/odata/ns/edm" Namespace="local">
           
            <Annotations Target="SAP.Ztest_ALP_ReportType">
                <Annotation Term="UI.Chart" Qualifier="Step1_CQ">
                    <Record Type="UI.ChartDefinitionType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="ChartType" EnumMember="UI.ChartType/Bar"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Title" String="Purchase Order Data"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Description" String="Purchase Order Data"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Dimensions">
                            <Collection>
                                <PropertyPath>Month_Year</PropertyPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="DimensionAttributes">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="UI.ChartDimensionAttributeType">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Dimension" PropertyPath="Month_Year"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Role" EnumMember="UI.ChartDimensionRoleType/Category"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Measures">
                            <Collection>
                                <PropertyPath>Amount</PropertyPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                        <PropertyValue Property="MeasureAttributes">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="UI.ChartMeasureAttributeType">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Measure" PropertyPath="Amount"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="Role" EnumMember="UI.ChartMeasureRoleType/Axis1"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>
                <Annotation Term="UI.PresentationVariant" Qualifier="Step2_pQ">
                    <Record Type="UI.PresentationVariantType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="Visualizations">
                            <Collection>
                                <AnnotationPath>@UI.Chart#Step1_CQ</AnnotationPath>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>
            </Annotations>
            <Annotations Target="SAP.Ztest_ALP_ReportType/Month_Year">
                <Annotation Term="Common.ValueList">
                    <Record Type="Common.ValueListType">
                        <PropertyValue Property="CollectionPath" String="Ztest_ALP_Report"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="PresentationVariantQualifier" String="Step2_pQ"/>
                        <PropertyValue Property="Parameters">
                            <Collection>
                                <Record Type="Common.ValueListParameterInOut">
                                    <PropertyValue Property="LocalDataProperty" PropertyPath="Month_Year"/>
                                    <PropertyValue Property="ValueListProperty" String="Month_Year"/>
                                </Record>
                            </Collection>
                        </PropertyValue>
                    </Record>
                </Annotation>
            </Annotations>
           
           
        </Schema>
    </edmx:DataServices>
</edmx:Edmx>


Conclusion:  In conclusion, Visual Filters in SAP Fiori Elements provide an intuitive way to filter and analyze data using graphical charts. They help users quickly understand data trends and apply filters easily, improving both usability and efficiency.

 

Thank You,

B Naresh Kumar Nayak

entry.title: Upload binary content via Cloud Integration to SAP with BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB
entry.content:
SAP Generic Object Services (GOS) is commonly used to store attachments for business objects such as sales orders, purchase orders, or deliveries.

In SAP PI/PO, many implementations use the RFC function module BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB to upload binary content to SAP. However, when working with SAP Cloud Integration, handling binary content requires a slightly different approach.

In this blog post, I will demonstrate how to build a simple iFlow that uploads a file from an external system to SAP and stores it as a GOS attachment using the RFC function module BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB.

Scenario Overview

The scenario works as follows:

  • A file is uploaded to an SFTP server.
  • Cloud Integration processes the file and converts it into the format required by BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB.
  • The RFC adapter calls the SAP backend system via Cloud Connector.
  • The document is stored in SAP and can be accessed via transaction OAOR.

Below is the overall iFlow design.

Processing Steps

Step 1 – Base64 Encode the File

The file content is first Base64 encoded. This prevents issues caused by special characters that may interfere with XML processing in later steps.

Step 2 – Prepare the XML Structure

Next, we prepare an XML message that will be used as the input for message mapping. 

Example structure:

<MT_File>
	<FileName>${header.CamelFileName}</FileName>
	<FileSize>${header.CamelFileLength}</FileSize>
	<Content>${body}</Content>
</MT_File>

Step 3 – Convert XML into RFC Request

The XML message is then mapped into the RFC structure required by the function module BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB. Example request:

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<ns1:BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB xmlns:ns1="urn:sap-com:document:sap:rfc:functions">
	<OBJECT_KEY>0000000001</OBJECT_KEY>
	<BINARY_FLAG>X</BINARY_FLAG>
	<CLASSNAME>SOFFICEINTEGRATION</CLASSNAME>
	<CLASSTYPE>OT</CLASSTYPE>
	<COMPONENTS>
		<item>
			<DOC_COUNT>00000000</DOC_COUNT>
			<MIMETYPE>text/plain</MIMETYPE>
			<COMP_SIZE>1957</COMP_SIZE>
		</item>
	</COMPONENTS>
	<CONTENT>
		<item>
			<LINE>446965204DC3BC...E62726F74</LINE>
		</item>
		<item>
			<LINE>656E20756E...CC39FC3A4</LINE>
		</item>
	</CONTENT>
	<SIGNATURE>
		<item>
			<DOC_COUNT>00000000</DOC_COUNT>
			<PROP_NAME>BDS_DOCUMENTCLASS</PROP_NAME>
			<PROP_VALUE>TXT</PROP_VALUE>
		</item>
		<item>
			<DOC_COUNT>00000000</DOC_COUNT>
			<PROP_NAME>BDS_DOCUMENTTYPE</PROP_NAME>
			<PROP_VALUE>TXT</PROP_VALUE>
		</item>
		<item>
			<DOC_COUNT>00000000</DOC_COUNT>
			<PROP_NAME>DESCRIPTION</PROP_NAME>
			<PROP_VALUE>german_text.txt</PROP_VALUE>
		</item>
	</SIGNATURE>
</ns1:BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB>

Important Parameters 

OBJECT_KEY: The identifier of the business object. For example, for a purchase order it would be like 4500000001, for delivery it would be like 0034423362. 

BINARY_FLAG: Set to X because the request contains binary data.

CLASSNAME: Defines the GOS class for the object. For example, `EKKO` would be for pruchase order, `LIKP` would be for delivery. 

CLASSTYPE: 'BO' for business object, 'OT' for others

COMPONENTS/item/MIMETYPE: its value is determined based on file extension, for example, 

  • .txt:  text/plain
  • .pdf: application/pdf
  • .xml: application/xml

COMPONENTS/item/COMP_SIZE: this is the file size. Map its value from header CamelFileLength. 

CONTENT/item & CONTENT/item/LINE: This section contains the actual binary content of the file. The content must be split into multiple lines because the RFC structure expects the data in chunks.

Some custom UDFs are used in the message mapping to handle binary processing.

import com.sap.it.api.mapping.Output;

def String getHexValue(String arg1){
    String hex = arg1.bytes.collect { String.format("%02X", it) }.join('')
    
	return hex 
}

def String getFileExtension(String fileName){
    def extension = fileName.substring(fileName.lastIndexOf('.') + 1).toUpperCase()
	return extension 
}

def String getByteSize(String ediString){
    Integer hexSize = ediString.bytes.size()
    
	return hexSize + '' 
}

def String base64Decode(String input) {
    def base64String = input.replaceAll("\\s", "")
    def base64Decoded = Base64.decoder.decode(base64String)
    
    return new String(base64Decoded)
}

def void splitBinaryContent(String[] input, String[] interval, Output output) {
    Integer intv = interval[0].toInteger()
    String s = input[0]
    
    Integer arrayLength = (int) Math.ceil(((s.bytes.size() / (double) intv)));
    String[] result = new String[arrayLength];
    
    int j = 0;
    int lastIndex = result.length - 1;
    for (int i = 0; i < lastIndex; i++) {
        byte[] subArray = Arrays.copyOfRange(s.bytes, j, j + intv);
        result[i] = new String(subArray, "UTF-8")
        j += intv;
        output.addValue(result[i]);
    }
    
    byte[] lastSubArray = Arrays.copyOfRange(s.bytes, j, s.bytes.size());
    result[lastIndex] = new String(lastSubArray, "UTF-8");
    output.addValue(result[lastIndex]);
}

Testing the Integration

After deploying the iFlow:

  • Upload a file to an SFTP server.
  • The iFlow processes the request.
  • The RFC adapter sends the request to SAP.
  • The document is stored successfully.

The attachment can then be viewed in SAP using transaction OAOR.

Conclusion

In this blog post, we demonstrated how to upload binary files from SAP Cloud Integration to SAP using the RFC function module BDS_BUSINESSDOCUMENT_CREA_TAB.

Key takeaways:

  • Binary content should be Base64 encoded before XML processing.
  • The content must be converted and split into chunks for the RFC structure.
  • Custom UDFs help with encoding, size calculation, and content splitting.

This approach can be used to attach documents to various SAP business objects via GOS. If you have questions or improvements, feel free to share them in the comments.

entry.title: Building a Scalable Micro-Frontend Dashboard with SAPUI5 Integration Cards
entry.content:
Introduction

Building an enterprise-grade analytics dashboard is challenging. When you add requirements like multi-team development, independent deployment cycles, and the need for extensibility, traditional monolithic approaches quickly become unmanageable.

This article shares our proposal building the Supply Chain Performance Dashboard at SAP—a micro-frontend architecture that supports dozens of independent KPI widgets, dynamic filtering, cross-widget interactions, and continuous deployment—all while maintaining team autonomy and code quality.

This article shares practical patterns for building a micro-frontend dashboard architecture that supports:

  • Dozens of independent KPI widgets
  • Dynamic filtering across all widgets
  • Cross-widget interactions
  • Continuous deployment with team autonomy

Whether you're building analytics dashboards, composable UIs, or exploring micro-frontend patterns, this deep-dive offers practical insights backed by real-world architectural decisions.

The Challenge: Complex Dashboard, Multiple Teams

An enterprise analytics dashboard that provides managers with real-time visibility into business metrics across multiple dimensions:

┌────────────────────────────────────────┐
│   Dashboard                            │
│                                        │
│  [View Tab 1] [View Tab 2] [Tab 3]     │  ← Multiple views
│  [Dimension A ⚡ Dimension B]           │  ← Toggle options
│                                        │
│  ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐ ┌─────────┐   │
│  │  KPI 1  │ │  KPI 2  │ │  KPI 3  │   │  ← Independent widgets
│  └─────────┘ └─────────┘ └─────────┘   │
│  ┌─────────────────┐ ┌─────────────┐   │
│  │    Chart 1      │ │   Table 1   │   │
│  └─────────────────┘ └─────────────┘   │
└────────────────────────────────────────┘

Requirements:

  • 40+ KPI widgets across multiple views × multiple dimensions
  • Multiple teams developing widgets independently
  • Continuous deployment without blocking other teams
  • Dynamic filters affecting all widgets simultaneously
  • Cross-filtering when users click chart segments
  • Grid customization (drag-drop, resize, hide/show)

Why Traditional Approaches Fail:

  • Monolithic SPA: Every widget change requires full redeployment
  • Tight coupling: One broken widget blocks entire dashboard
  • Single pipeline: Multiple teams competing for deployment window

We needed true micro-frontend architecture.

 

Architecture Decision 1: Repository Structure

The Question

Should the dashboard shell and KPI widgets live in one repository or separate repositories?

Option A: Monorepo

analytics-dashboard/          ← Single repo
├── dashboard-shell/
│   ├── webapp/
│   └── ui5.yaml
└── widgets/
    ├── widget-a/
    ├── widget-b/
    └── ...

Pros: Simple setup, single CI/CD pipeline
Cons: Deployment coupling, merge conflicts, repository grows massive

 

Option B: Two Repositories - Selected

dashboard-shell/           ← Repo 1: Orchestration layer
├── webapp/
│   ├── config/layout.json
│   ├── controller/Dashboard.controller.ts
│   ├── model/LayoutManager.ts
│   └── view/Dashboard.view.xml

widget-registry/          ← Repo 2: All widgets + discovery service
├── widgets/
│   ├── widget-a/
│   ├── widget-b/
│   └── ...
├── registry-service/
│   └── widget-registry.json
└── build.sh

Contract Between Repositories: Repository Exposes Consumes

Discovers widgets
Loads assets dynamically

 

Why We Chose Two Repositories:

Independent deployment cycles:

  • Dashboard updates don't require widget releases
  • Widget teams deploy on their own schedule
  • Broken widget doesn't block dashboard

Clear ownership boundaries

  • Dashboard team owns orchestration
  • Widget teams own visualizations
  • Contract enforces decoupling

Scalable CI/CD

  • Two focused pipelines vs one massive pipeline
  • Faster builds (only changed repo rebuilds)
  • Parallel development without conflicts

Trade-off: Slightly more complex integration testing, but worth it for deployment flexibility.

 

Architecture Decision 2: Integration Strategy

The Question

How should the dashboard load and render independent widgets at runtime?

Evaluated Options

Option A: Iframe-Based 

<iframe src="https://widgets.example.com/widget-a"></iframe>

Pros:Complete isolation
Cons: Performance overhead, difficult communication, styling inconsistency, accessibility issues

 

Option B: Native Web Components

customElements.define('analytics-widget', class extends HTMLElement {});

Pros: Browser-native, framework-agnostic
Cons: No SAPUI5 features, reinvent data binding/routing/i18n, steep learning curve

 

Option C: UI5 Components

const oComponent = await Component.create({ name: "com.example.widget" });

Pros: Full SAPUI5 capabilities
Cons: Complex lifecycle management, no standard metadata format

 

Option D : UI5 Integration Cards

Manifest-based configuration:

{
    "sap.app": {
        "id": "com.example.ordervalue",
        "type": "card"
    },
    "sap.card": {
        "type": "Component",
        "header": {
            "title": "Order Value at Risk"
        },
        "content": {
            "componentName": "com.example.ordervalue"
        }
    }
}

Dashboard loads dynamically:

const oCard = new Card({
    manifest: "https://widgets.example.com/order-value/manifest.json",
    baseUrl: "https://widgets.example.com/order-value"
});
oCard.placeAt("container");

 

Why Integration Cards: 

Standardized format - Official SAPUI5 specification

Support Two card types:

  • Component cards: Full custom code for complex widgets
  • Declarative cards: Manifest-only (List, Table, Analytical, ...)

Built-in features: Headers, data binding, actions, error handling

Parameter passing: card.setParameters() with auto-refresh

Framework alignment: Native SAPUI5, team expertise, Fiori guidelines

 

Architecture Decision 3: Communication Patterns

The Challenge: Bidirectional Communication

Two-way communication required:

  1. Dashboard → Widgets: Apply filters to all widgets
  2. Widgets → Dashboard: Handle user clicks (cross-filtering, navigation)

 

Evaluated Patterns

Pattern 1: Pure EventBus

eventBus.publish("filters", "changed", { timeRange: "last30days" });

Pros: Loose coupling
Cons: State scattered in events

 

Pattern 2: State-Driven Reactive 

const filterModel = new JSONModel({ timeRange: "last30days" });

Pros: Single source of truth
Cons: Integration Cards don't support reactive binding

 

Pattern 3: Host-Controlled

cards.forEach(card => card.refresh());

Pros: Centralized control
Cons: Tight coupling

 

Our Solution: Hybrid EventBus + State-Driven

Best of both worlds:

// 1. Centralized State (Single Source of Truth)
const filterModel = new JSONModel({
    timeRange: null,
    category: [],
    status: []
});
this.setModel(filterModel, "filters");

// 2. FilterBar updates state + publishes event
onSearch() {
    const filters = this.getFilterValues();
    
    // Update centralized state
    this.getModel("filters").setData(filters);
    
    // Publish notification event
    eventBus.publish("app.filters", "changed", {
        timestamp: new Date().toISOString()
    });
}

// 3. Dashboard reads state + updates cards
onFiltersChanged() {
    // Read from centralized state (not event payload)
    const filters = this.getModel("filters").getData();
    
    // Update all cards
    this._cards.forEach(card => {
        card.setParameters(filters);
        card.refresh();
    });
}

Pros:

Single Source of Truth: Centralized filter state

Explicit Notifications: EventBus for change observation

Universal Support: Works with Component and Declarative cards

Debuggable: Inspect model state + trace events

Testable: Mock state and events independently

 

Widget-to-Dashboard Communication

Widgets fire action events on user interaction

{
    "sap.card": {
        "type": "List",
        "content": {
            "item": {
                "title": "{name}",
                "actions": [
                    {
                        "type": "Custom",
                        "parameters": {
                            "id": "{id}",
                            "action": "viewDetails",
                            "type": "ItemSelected",
                            "payload": {
                                "name": "{name}",
                                "id": "{id}"
                            }
                        }
                    }
                ]
            }
        }
    }
}

Flow:

  1. User clicks item in widget
  2. Widget fires action event
  3. Dashboard receives event
  4. Dashboard updates filter state
  5. Dashboard publishes filter change
  6. All widgets refresh with new filter

 

Lessons Learned

What Worked Well

Two-repository approach

  • True team independence
  • No merge conflicts

Deploy anytime without coordination

SAPUI5 Integration Cards

  • Standardized pattern
  • Supports Component and Declarative types
  • Built-in features reduce boilerplate

Hybrid communication

  • Centralized state = single source of truth
  • EventBus = loose coupling
  • Works with all card types

Contract-based development

  • Registry acts as interface definition
  • Tags enable dynamic filtering
  • Clear responsibilities

 

Results & Key Takeaways

Achieved:

  • 40+ widgets by 10+ teams
  • Independent deployment cycles
  • Zero downtime deployments

Key Insights:

  • Micro-frontends are organizational—they're about team autonomy and deployment independence, not just technology.
  • Match patterns to constraints—we chose Integration Cards because teams knew UI5, not because it's universally "best."
  • Centralize wisely—filters are centralized (single source of truth), implementation is distributed (team autonomy).
  • Contracts are your API—treat the widget registry with the same rigor as REST APIs.

 

Resources

SAPUI5 Documentation:

  • UI5 Integration Cards
  • EventBus API
  • Component Cards

Micro-frontend Patterns:

  • Micro Frontends by Martin Fowler

entry.title: Re: Resource Handover for Product WTs in S/4HANA Cloud Pu...
entry.content:
Hello @AdamT89

No, you must not always use HUs for every such movement. Layout-oriented storage control with intermediate bins is viable, and this is the standard way to model a physical handover when you process warehouse task.

Here is more information:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/6782e8a001d64462a6ec6dd0ac39818c.html?locale=en-US 

So, if your goal is to retain the replenishment process and still allow execution in two physical steps by different resources, then LOSC is the one you should evaluate first.

See more details in the reference below.

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/87f9b54f9c4f4e75aff0061860a6589a/0bfbc5fe6cfb4a34af90f72307386eba.html?locale=en-US 

From an operational standpoint, HUs can make the physical handover cleaner. However, I would not say, the HU based LOSC is the only possible option. Public Cloud also supports product warehouse task, and you can do picking without a pick HU.

See the following reference for more insights

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c46da892efa14cea8347e9b872199d6f/07bcf774644344b8a80c943978791ba6.html?locale=en-US 

I suggest you keep replenishment functionality and introduce a controlled handover, LOSC with an intermediate bin is the primary standard design you should evaluate.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Customize SAPUI5 apps for business users – make an impact by joining our CEI project
entry.content:
Hi @K4I,

such behaviour (individual elements are removed either in the Adaptation Editor or via key user adaptation, then reappear on next application) is often due to controller coding affecting the visibility of controls. The visibility of controls should either by controlled by controller coding, or by key user adaptation (via add/remove changes), but not both, as stated in our documentation. Looking at the controller coding for app F2072 I can see multiple calls to setVisible for the mass edit button, so I'm assuming this is what is happening here.

It is up to the app developers to decide if they want to support this scenario (removing the mass edit button via key user adaptation / adaptation project), but if they choose not to, they should adapt the app accordingly. Also, they will probably be able to provide a way to hide the mass edit button without an adaptation project / key user adaptation. Please create an incident to component PM-FIO. 

Best regards,
Dominik

entry.title: Re: Production order overhead cost using BOM
entry.content:
Hi @shihab_muriyankarasiyadh ,
My friend you cannot directly add cost inside the BOM. SAP does not support adding non‑material overhead items directly to a BOM, but you can achieve the same costing effect using a costing sheet or a non‑stock material with special settings. SAP BOMs only support materials and document items. They cannot contain pure cost elements or overhead objects.
This is why SAP normally uses Activity types in routing, Template allocation and Costing sheets

 

Regards

entry.title: Re: Unit of Measurement: EUR / Euro (CUNI)
entry.content:
Hi @AlexSP,
My friend you should not create “EURO” (EUR) as a Unit of Measurement in CUNI. In SAP, currency is never maintained as a unit of measure, and EUR already exists in the system as a currency key, not a UoM. Using EUR as a UoM would break SAP design principles and cause issues in CO, FI, and reporting.

SAP separates Units of Measure (UoM) and Currencies. UoM (CUNI) is for quantities (kg, m, pcs, hours). Currencies (OB08 / TCUR)* are for monetary values (EUR, USD, SAR).
SAP does not allow or recommend mixing these domains. Creating EUR as a UoM would violate this architecture and confuse downstream processes.

entry.title: Re: Datasphere: Accessing target structure in Python Script
entry.content:
Hello @mitko1994 ,

The short answer to your question is: No.
The Python editor is incredible simple, and is executed in it's own environment, separate from the data flow in general.  

Another fun note: the dataflow matches your columns on the order in the dataframe, not by texts or keys. 

So, not only can't you access the target structure - you have to manually create it, and make sure the output of your script matches the output exactly. 

Sorry I can't be more helpful 😞

entry.title: Using Claude Code and Natural Language to Create SAP Datasphere Artifacts
entry.content:
How It Started

A few weeks ago, I tried an AI tool called Trae_CN to create Datasphere artifacts automatically. After a few hours of testing, it worked well! I was excited and wrote a blog post about it.

AI-Assisted SAP Datasphere Modeling

Then my teams chat filled up with messages from the security team.

The problem? Trae_CN wasn't approved by the company.

So I switched to Claude Code and tried again. This time, I wanted to solve a harder problem: creating complex models automatically. I focused on Analytic Models - the kind that everyone needs but finds hard to automate. These models need many steps: linking tables, setting up measures, and configuring business layers. Usually, you have to do all this by hand in the UI.

If we can use natural language to create these complex models, that would really save time.

I. The Problem: Too Much Manual Work

Data engineers spend a lot of time creating artifacts in SAP Datasphere.

When projects get big:

  • One data warehouse: 50+ tables, 30+ views
  • Complex analytic models: 10+ linked tables

Each artifact needs manual work: open UI → fill in fields → set up links → save

One table takes 5 minutes. 50 tables = 4 hours.

And many of these objects follow the same patterns. They can be automated.

II. The Idea: Use Natural Language

The ideal way should be like this:

"Create a customer table with ID, name, email, and city"
"Create a view from the customer table, show only ID and name"
"Create an analytic model linking customer and product tables"

Just say what you need, and it's done automatically.

III. The Solution: Claude Code + Skills + Datasphere CLI

How It Works

Claude Code understands your words and calls Skills that connect to Datasphere CLI to create artifacts.

The steps:

  1. You describe what you need in plain language
  2. Claude Code understands what you mean
  3. It calls the right Skill
  4. The Skill connects to Datasphere using SAP's official @sap/datasphere-cli tool
  5. It makes the JSON and creates the artifact

An Example

You say:

"Create a customer table with ID, name, and email. ID is the key."

Claude Code runs:

/create-local-table --name CUSTOMER --columns ID:String:10:key,NAME:String:100,EMAIL:String:100

The Skill creates this JSON:

{
  "definitions": {
    "CUSTOMER": {
      "kind": "entity",
      "elements": {
        "ID": { "type": "cds.String", "length": 10, "key": true },
        "NAME": { "type": "cds.String", "length": 100 },
        "EMAIL": { "type": "cds.String", "length": 100 }
      }
    }
  }
}

Done.

Why Natural Language Works

You can say it different ways:

  • "Make a table with order number, amount, and date"
  • "I need a table for customer information"
  • "Create a sales data table"

Claude Code understands all of these and calls the right Skill.

The Big Challenge: No Format Guide

Problem: Datasphere CLI docs don't show you how to write the JSON format.

What to do?

Let AI learn it.

The method is simple:

  1. Create an example artifact in Datasphere UI by hand
  2. Use CLI to read that object's full structure
  3. Let Claude Code look at the JSON and find the patterns
  4. Use that template to make new artifacts
# Read an existing artifact
datasphere objects views read --name EXISTING_VIEW

# Claude Code helps figure out:
# - What fields are required?
# - What can we skip?
# - What are the rules?

This "reverse engineering" method helped me learn the right format for all artifact types.

IV. What I Built: 6 Skills, Focus on Analytic Models

The Skills I made:

Skill What It Does

create-local-tableMakes tables
create-viewMakes views
create-analytic-modelMakes analytic models
create-data-flowMakes data flows
create-replication-flowMakes replication flows
create-transformation-flowMakes transformation flows

Analytic Models

Why focus on this one? Because it's the most complex.

Analytic models need:

  • Linking fact and dimension tables
  • Setting up measure calculations
  • Choosing dimension fields
  • Setting up business layers

The old way to create an analytic model:

  1. Drag dimension tables
  2. Set up link fields
  3. Pick dimension fields
  4. Define measures
  5. Save and deploy

Takes 10-20 minutes.

The new way:

/create-analytic-model --name AM_SALES \
  --source SALES_FACT \
  --measures AMOUNT:sum,QUANTITY:sum \
  --dimensions CUSTOMER_ID:DIM_CUSTOMER:ID:NAME,CITY;PRODUCT_ID:DIM_PRODUCT:ID:NAME,CATEGORY

Done in 30 seconds.

It does this automatically:

  • Links customer dimension (using CUSTOMER_ID)
  • Links product dimension (using PRODUCT_ID)
  • Adds the dimension fields you want (NAME, CITY, CATEGORY)
  • Sets up measure calculations (sum of AMOUNT and QUANTITY)

V. Real Results: Talk in Natural Language

Creating a Sales Analysis Example

You tell Claude Code:

"Create a sales analysis data model for me.

First, make a sales fact table with order number, customer ID, product ID, and amount.

Then make a customer dimension table with ID, name, and city.

Next, make a product dimension table with ID, name, and category.

Finally, make an analytic model based on the sales fact table, linking these two dimensions, with amount sum as the measure."

Claude Code does this:

# 1. Create sales fact table
/create-local-table --name SALES_FACT \
  --columns ORDER_ID:String:10:key,CUSTOMER_ID:String:10,PRODUCT_ID:String:10,AMOUNT:Decimal:15:2

# 2. Create customer dimension table
/create-local-table --name DIM_CUSTOMER \
  --columns ID:String:10:key,NAME:String:100,CITY:String:50

# 3. Create product dimension table
/create-local-table --name DIM_PRODUCT \
  --columns ID:String:10:key,NAME:String:100,CATEGORY:String:50

# 4. Create analytic model
/create-analytic-model --name AM_SALES \
  --source SALES_FACT \
  --measures AMOUNT:sum \
  --dimensions CUSTOMER_ID:DIM_CUSTOMER:ID:NAME,CITY;PRODUCT_ID:DIM_PRODUCT:ID:NAME,CATEGORY

What happens:

  • You describe what you need in plain language
  • Claude Code understands and breaks it into steps
  • It calls the right Skills
  • Your whole data model is built

Time to build:

  • Old way: 2-3 hours
  • Natural language + Claude Code: 1 minute

VI. Open Source Project

The code is on GitHub: https://github.com/yuyonggang/dsp-cli

What's included:

  • 6 complete Skills
  • Full instructions (in English and Chinese)
  • Setup guide
  • Security guide

You can use it as is or change it for your needs.

I built this whole project using Claude Code — AI helped from start to finish.

Final Thoughts

What to Know

1. This Is Still New

Right now, the Skills work best for simple models. For complex business cases:

  • You need to test and try things
  • You might need to fix problems by hand
  • The Skills need more work

This is a step-by-step process, not instant.

2. Be Careful with Big Projects

If your project is complex:

  • Start with simple cases to test
  • Move to harder models slowly
  • Leave time for fixing issues

What's Next

My goal:

Give it a design doc → It creates everything

For example, give an AI Agent tool (like Claude Code) a requirements document:

Sales Analysis System Design
- Sales fact table: order, customer, product, amount, date
- Customer dimension: ID, name, city, country
- Product dimension: ID, name, category, price
- Analytic model: multi-dimension sales analysis

The AI Agent would:

  1. Read the document
  2. Create all tables and dimensions
  3. Build analytic models
  4. Hit an error? Read the error message
  5. Check API docs to understand why
  6. Fix it and try again

Fully automatic, no human help needed.

Using AI Agents to understand needs, work on their own, and fix their own mistakes in Datasphere projects.
